findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC (or a dedicated 'Testing plan' section) that covers: (a) unit tests for each Layer-1 check with pass/fail/skip fixture tickets; (b) contract tests for Layer-2 verdict schema; (c) integration test asserting claim blocks without attestation and passes with a valid one; (d) mutation test that a material edit invalidates the cert; (e) reducer test that REVIEW_RESULT events are skipped. Specify RED-before-GREEN ordering for the claim integration path.

findings.location: Context section — first two paragraphs

AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points' and Design summary: 'a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'criteria registry' (declarative descriptors + generic orchestrator) and a per-criterion config knob system. Without access to the live codebase it is impossible to verify whether rebar.llm's existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner already subsumes these needs or whether a genuinely new abstraction layer is required. If the existing extension points are as capable as implied, the registry + orchestrator may be a thin wrapper used only here (Rule-of-Three risk). The plan's stated intent is to reuse those extension points, which is the right posture, but the degree of new abstraction is unverifiable from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief note in the design summary or AC that explicitly states which existing rebar.llm interfaces are being extended vs. which new interfaces are being introduced, and why any new interface must exist (e.g., 'frozen-within-loop convergence requires a registry not already present in catalog/contracts'). This makes the NIH boundary explicit and reviewable.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section pairs: (1) Design summary 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type' vs. AC 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' and AC item 'Bugs are exempt from this gate'; (2) Design summary 'LAYER 1 … BLOCKS' vs. AC 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented … fail-open'; (3) Design summary 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)' vs. AC 'per-plan budget cap … INDETERMINATE outcome (non-blocking default, configurable)'
findings.finding:
Three tension points are worth flagging, though none rises to a hard contradiction:
1. The design routing rule ('pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type') appears to conflict with the bug-exemption rule ('Bugs are exempt from this gate'). Ticket type IS used — just exclusively for bugs. This is a latent inconsistency that could confuse implementers about whether type-based routing is or isn't allowed.
2. 'LAYER 1 BLOCKS' (design) vs. 'fail-open' (AC layer-1 item): the plan intends both to be true simultaneously (blocks on supported stacks, fail-open on unsupported), but the AC phrasing 'fail-open: readiness shape … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)' lists these together without distinguishing which checks are fail-open and which are hard-blocking. A reader could misread 'readiness shape' as also fail-open when it is apparently always-blocking.
3. The design says 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' but the AC says 'INDETERMINATE outcome (non-blocking default)'. AMBIGUOUS and INDETERMINATE appear to be different states (AMBIGUOUS = needs escalation; INDETERMINATE = budget/tool unavailable), but the relationship between them is not defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer could conflate or mis-implement them.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Restate the routing rule as: 'routing pivots on has_children + complexity; bug tickets are additionally exempt by type before routing'. 2. In the Layer-1 AC bullet, split the checks into two sub-lists: (a) always-blocking structural checks and (b) fail-open tool-backed checks. 3. Add a one-sentence definition distinguishing AMBIGUOUS (verdict state = escalate) from INDETERMINATE (review-level state = budget/tool unavailable) so implementers don't conflate the two.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'
findings.finding:
Two acceptance criteria are not verifiable within one working session and rely on out-of-session evidence:
1. SC-3 requires that findings 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'. Measuring improvement in agent plan quality requires a before/after experiment across a corpus of real tickets — this cannot be demonstrated in a single implementation session. No proxy metric or in-session test harness is defined.
2. The AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' defers correctness of core configuration defaults to an external experiment corpus referenced only by session log ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b'). If those experiments have not yet been run, or are not reproducible within the session, this criterion cannot be verified by the agent executing the ticket. It also makes the AC non-self-contained.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' in SC-3 with an in-session proxy: e.g., 'each advisory finding includes a grounded location, specific suggested edit, and severity ranking (validated by unit tests on the feedback-authoring module)'. Defer the empirical 'measurably better' claim to follow-on F10 (calibrated abstention + labeled gold set).
2. Either inline the key experiment conclusions (specific numeric defaults for chunking/budget/depth) directly into the AC, or restate the criterion as 'defaults are set to [specific values] as documented in [accessible artifact]; experiments to validate those values are tracked in follow-on Fx'. This makes the criterion self-contained and verifiable.

findings.location: Design summary ('Trigger / enforcement') vs. AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan states that the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND and that default chunking/budget/depth settings will be grounded in experiment results, but the experiment work is neither described in the plan nor confirmed to exist as a prior deliverable — it is merely referenced as 'linked experiment work'. If those experiments are part of this epic's scope, the AC is internally consistent; if they are a prerequisite that must already be complete, this could conflict with the AC that demands defaults be empirically grounded (they can't be if the experiments haven't run yet). Additionally, the plan says 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic' in the Design summary, which matches AC bullet 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' — no contradiction there. No other meaningful cross-section contradictions were found.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the AC or a 'Prerequisites' section whether the empirical experiments are (a) already completed and their results available, (b) part of a parallel workstream, or (c) to be run as part of this epic before finalizing defaults. If (c), add an explicit AC bullet for the experiment execution.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)' vs. entire plan body
AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding:
This criterion is not verifiable within the session because it depends on external experiment results that are only referenced by a session-log ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') and not reproduced or attached. An agent cannot confirm whether the defaults it ships actually match empirical findings without access to those results. The criterion is therefore unmeasurable in-session as written.

Additionally, the AC bullet 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal' (Success Criteria) verges on out-of-session validation: confirming that an external CI process can consume the attestation requires either a real CI pipeline or a clear simulation spec — neither is provided.

The remaining criteria are generally measurable (deterministic tool checks, event emission, config knobs, blocking behavior) and appear completable within a session given the described tooling.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiment-grounded defaults AC: either (a) inline the key empirical findings (or a summary table) so the agent can validate compliance without chasing an external log, or (b) reword to 'Defaults are documented with a rationale; empirical tuning is a follow-on (F-experiment)' and file that as a follow-on item. For the CI-check AC: add a concrete in-session test, e.g., 'a test script that reads the signed attestation from a fixture ticket and exits 0/non-0, passing CI-check acceptance without a live pipeline.'

findings.location: AC bullet: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented ... AC/DD quality, destructive sniff' vs. Design summary (no mention of 'DD quality' or 'destructive sniff' acronym expansion)
findings.finding:
Two minor issues:
1. **Orphan AC detail / unexpanded terminology**: The AC bullet lists 'AC/DD quality' and 'destructive sniff' as Layer-1 checks, but the Design summary never defines 'DD' (presumably 'Description/Design'?) or explains what a 'destructive sniff' entails. This is terminology used only in the AC, not in the body — a weak orphan.
2. **Potential near-duplicate between Success Criteria and AC**: Success Criterion 3 ('Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise') and AC bullet for feedback-authoring ('Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec') substantially overlap. This is not a true duplicate because one speaks to outcome and the other to process conformance, but they are close enough to risk being treated as the same check.

No true orphan ACs (every major AC bullet has a corresponding design section) and no described work entirely without a criterion were found.
findings.suggested_edit: Expand 'DD' in the AC bullet (e.g., 'AC/Description quality') and add a one-sentence definition of 'destructive sniff' (e.g., 'heuristic check for destructive operations — DROP TABLE, rm -rf — without safeguards') directly in that bullet or in a Glossary section. For the near-duplicate, consider merging SC-3 and the feedback-authoring AC, or explicitly call out that SC-3 is the outcome metric and the AC is the implementation conformance check.

findings.location: Design summary ('Trigger / enforcement') vs. AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data; per-plan budget cap' in the Design Summary, but delegates the actual defaults to experiment results that are referenced but not yet present in this plan ('see linked experiment work'). If the experiment work is not yet filed or completed, there is no concrete basis for the defaults, creating a dependency that is unresolved within the epic itself. This is not a strict contradiction but leaves the AC for defaults unverifiable until the experiment tickets are done. Additionally, the Design Summary states 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic' and the AC confirms 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' — these are consistent. However, the plan simultaneously says Layer-1 blocks while Layer-2 is advisory 'never blocks by default', yet a per-criterion config knob can make a judgment criterion 'BLOCKING'. The AC does list 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override' which aligns. No hard contradiction found, but the unresolved experiment-results dependency is a coherence gap worth flagging.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the empirically derived defaults (even as provisional values) so that AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' is self-contained, or explicitly note that this AC is conditional on a linked experiment sub-ticket being completed first and list that ticket as a prerequisite.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design Summary; Success Criteria; AC
AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: This acceptance criterion is not self-contained and cannot be verified within the session that implements this epic: it explicitly defers to 'the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' without specifying what those results are, where they live, or what values they produce. An agent cannot verify it has met this criterion without access to an external artifact that is not part of the plan. Similarly, AC bullet 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern' is measurable as a documentation checklist but its completeness is open-ended ('how the gate works' is broad). These are addressable but currently under-specified. The remainder of the ACs (Layer-1 checks, Layer-2 criteria, signing, REVIEW_RESULT event, claim integration, etc.) are generally agent-verifiable by inspection/test.
findings.suggested_edit: For the experiment-results AC: either (a) inline the provisional default values directly in the plan (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call, budget cap = X tokens, depth levels = Y') with a note that they may be updated post-experiment, making the criterion immediately verifiable; or (b) split this into a follow-on AC that is explicitly gated on the experiment ticket. For the docs AC: enumerate the specific doc sections required (e.g., a README section for each named topic) so an agent can tick them off.

findings.location: Design summary ('Polyglot tooling') vs. AC Layer-1 bullet; 'Follow-on work' section vs. AC
findings.finding: 1. The Design Summary mentions 'scc + lizard (size)' for oversize signals and 'solidlsp + ast-grep' for resolution, but the Layer-1 AC bullet lists 'readiness shape, file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff' without explicitly naming scc/lizard. This is a minor terminology inconsistency between the tooling named in Design and the AC — not a contradiction, but an agent may miss the tool binding without cross-referencing. 2. The Follow-on section lists 'F11 rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers' as out of scope, yet SC bullet 6 states 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal' and AC includes 'Docs: ... the CI-check pattern'. The plan implies some CI-check capability is in scope (documentation at minimum) while F11 defers the 'helpers'. This boundary is ambiguous — it is unclear whether any executable CI integration is in scope or only documentation. 3. No orphan criteria found: all AC items map to described work. No described work appears entirely uncovered by an AC.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. In the Layer-1 AC bullet, append '(scc + lizard for size)' to 'oversize signals' to match the Design Summary terminology and remove ambiguity for the implementing agent. 2. Clarify the CI scope boundary: add a parenthetical to the CI AC such as '(documentation and attestation format only; executable CI integration helpers are F11)'.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner).'
findings.finding: The plan calls for a 'criteria registry' with a 'generic orchestrator' driven by declarative descriptors. Whether this is NIH / gratuitous abstraction depends entirely on whether rebar.llm already provides a registrable, declarative criterion descriptor pattern that can be reused here without a new layer. If rebar.llm's existing extension points already handle this, the plan's claim of 'reuse' holds and there is no over-engineering concern. If the 'generic orchestrator' is a new dispatch layer wrapping a single call-site (this gate only), the Rule-of-Three is violated. Cannot determine from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence in the Design summary clarifying whether the criteria-registry/orchestrator pattern is already used in the completion-verifier (and therefore this is a true extension of an existing N-site abstraction) or whether it is newly introduced for this gate alone. If the latter, note that the abstraction should be introduced only if F7/F8 follow-ons are in-scope.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section tensions; see findings below.
findings.finding:
Three cross-section tensions worth flagging, none clearly contradictory but each needs a benign-reading confirmation:

1. TRIGGER MODEL vs. DEFAULTS: The Design summary says 'heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' (implying the claim is instant), while AC item 6 says 'Configurable chunking … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome'. Out-of-band execution and chunk-atomic checkpointing are compatible, but the plan never states what happens if the out-of-band review hasn't finished yet when `claim` is called — does `claim` block, time-out with INDETERMINATE, or pass optimistically? The trigger section says 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation', which implies the review must already be complete before claim. That is internally consistent only if the review is always pre-computed (triggered separately), but the plan never says what triggers the out-of-band review.

2. BUGS EXEMPT vs. ROUTING LOGIC: 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic' appears in both the design summary and AC item 10 (consistent), but the routing section pivots on 'has_children + complexity', with no mention of ticket type as a routing dimension. Bug exemption would require type-based routing, which is absent from the routing description.

3. LAYER-1 BLOCKS vs. FAIL-OPEN IRONCLAD: Layer-1 is described as 'BLOCKS' (frozen, deterministic), and simultaneously the fail-open rule says any unsupported lang/tool/crash/timeout → skipped = PASS. These are compatible in principle, but AC item 1 says 'readiness shape (standardized ## Acceptance Criteria across all types)' is a Layer-1 blocking check — structural/textual, not tool-dependent, so it would fire on bugs too, contradicting the bug-exempt rule. This is a latent contradiction that could cause implementation confusion.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add a sentence under 'Trigger / enforcement' stating what triggers the out-of-band review (e.g. a webhook/event on ticket creation or plan-edit) and what `claim` does if no attestation exists yet (expected answer: blocks with 'run plan-review first'). 2. Add ticket-type as an explicit routing dimension (bug → skip entire gate, not just this epic's scope). 3. Clarify whether the 'readiness shape' Layer-1 check applies to bug tickets or is also skipped when the bug-exemption fires.

findings.location: Success Criteria item 3; AC items 2, 6, 11.
findings.finding:
Three acceptance criteria are not concretely measurable or are explicitly deferred to out-of-session work:

1. 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that MEASURABLY HELP an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion 3). 'Measurably help' is not verifiable within a single working session — it requires FP/remediation-rate data from field use, which is explicitly deferred to Follow-on F1. The AC as written cannot be checked by an agent at implementation time.

2. 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' (AC item 11). This ties a shipped AC to external experiments that are not part of this epic's deliverables and may not be complete. An agent cannot verify this criterion without access to and completion of those experiments, which constitutes out-of-session validation.

3. 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern' (AC item 12). Documentation completeness is subjective without a checklist of required sections or a minimum coverage definition. As written it is unmeasurable ('what counts as adequate docs?').
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a session-checkable proxy, e.g. 'each advisory finding references a criterion id, a quoted location, a severity, and a concrete suggested edit — verifiable by inspection of sample outputs from a test fixture'. 2. Either include the experiment work as a sub-task of this epic with a concrete deliverable (e.g. 'a table of tested chunk-sizes and their latency/quality trade-offs'), or reword AC 11 to 'chunking/budget/depth defaults are documented with a rationale; experiments are a follow-on'. 3. Expand AC 12 into an enumerated checklist of required doc sections (trigger mechanics, config keys + types + defaults, fail-open matrix, attestation schema, CI-check command example) so completeness is binary-checkable.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)' and 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding:
Two potential anti-slop risks are present but cannot be fully confirmed without the live codebase:

1. **Orchestrator reuse vs. new layer:** The plan says to 'reuse rebar.llm extension points' for the criteria registry + orchestrator, but also calls for a new 'declarative descriptor' registry and 'generic orchestrator'. Whether this is true reuse or a new abstraction riding atop an existing one-call-site mechanism depends on how rebar.llm's catalog/contracts are currently structured. If the existing catalog already has a registry contract, the new descriptor format is justified. If not, this introduces a new pattern used at exactly one site.

2. **Tool proliferation:** Four distinct external tools (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard) are mandated for Layer-1. If rebar already wraps any of these for other gates (e.g., the completion-verifier), the integrations are reuse. If all four are new integrations, that is a wide new dependency surface. The plan does note solidlsp is 'already in repo', but the status of ast-grep, syft, scc, and lizard is unconfirmed.

The plan's stated rationale for fail-open and polyglot coverage justifies the breadth, so this is unlikely to be gratuitous — but the live codebase would need to confirm (a) how much of the orchestrator is truly reused and (b) which tools are already integrated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief note in the design summary stating which tools are already integrated in the repo (beyond solidlsp) and which are net-new dependencies. For the orchestrator, explicitly name the rebar.llm extension point(s) being reused so reviewers can verify no new one-site abstraction is introduced.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet points) and Follow-on work section; the word 'test' or 'testing' does not appear anywhere in the ACs or the Design summary.
findings.finding:
The plan contains no testing plan. There is no mention of:
- How the Layer-1 deterministic criteria will be tested (unit tests, contract tests, fail-open boundary cases).
- How the Layer-2 advisory review output will be validated (does the coaching feedback conform to the spec? RED-before-GREEN for new criteria descriptors?).
- How attestation signing/invalidation logic will be tested (including edge cases: material vs. non-material edits, stale-signature detection).
- How the `claim` fast-path check will be tested under --force override.
- How the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event round-trip (emit → reducer skips → offline join) will be verified.
- Failure-path coverage: tool crashes, timeouts, unsupported languages, budget exhaustion → INDETERMINATE.

AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' gestures at empirical validation but describes a calibration exercise, not a test suite. The Docs AC is also purely documentation. Neither substitutes for behavioral test coverage.

The completion-verifier mentioned as the design analogue presumably has tests — their pattern could be referenced here but isn't.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC or sub-section titled 'Testing approach' covering at minimum: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check with at least one passing and one failing fixture per check, including a fail-open fixture for each tool (crash/timeout/unsupported-lang → PASS); (2) contract/integration tests for the attestation signing and invalidation logic; (3) a test confirming the reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT events without corrupting state; (4) end-to-end smoke test of the claim gate blocking on absent/stale attestation and passing on a valid one; (5) RED-before-GREEN confirmation that each new criterion is tested before implementation.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Execution: chunking … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and Success Criteria bullet 4: 'A project can configure risk posture, chunking, budget caps, and per-criterion blocking via config, with sensible defaults shipped.' and AC bullet 11: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
The end-state is broadly reachable from the described steps, and the AC list is thorough. One gap may prevent a clean done-signal:

**The experiment prerequisite is unresolved.** AC item 11 requires defaults to be 'grounded in experiment results (see linked experiment work)'. This implies the experiments are either already done or are a dependency of this epic. The plan does not clarify: (a) whether the experiments are in-scope work for this epic or a pre-condition already completed, (b) what 'done' looks like for the experiment phase before shipping defaults, and (c) what fallback defaults ship if experiments are not yet concluded.

If the experiments are in-scope but unplanned, the epic's end-state is unreachable without them, and an agent would not know when to stop. If they are already complete (session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b`), the plan should say so explicitly so the executor doesn't spin up new experiment work.

All other ACs map cleanly to described design components and appear reachable.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify AC 11: either 'experiments already completed in session 63cc… — use those results' or 'experiment work is a sub-task of this epic — file as a child ticket before implementation begins, with explicit exit criteria'. Add a brief fallback: 'if experiments are incomplete at ship time, use conservative defaults (e.g., one criterion per LLM call, N-token budget cap) and mark as configurable.'

findings.location: Multiple locations: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; (2) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; (3) 'extends the close-gate signing'; (4) 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event … reducer skips the type'; (5) 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity'; (6) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'
findings.finding:
The plan rests on at least six unverified assertions about existing codebase capabilities that an executor would need to confirm before relying on them:

1. **solidlsp is 'already in repo'** — assumed callable; its current API surface, supported language coverage, and crash/timeout contract are unverified from this plan alone.
2. **rebar.llm has catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points** that can be reused as-is for the criteria orchestrator — if the API doesn't match the descriptor model, the orchestrator is a net-new build.
3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — assumes the close-gate attestation subsystem has an extensible signing API. If it is monolithic, 'extending' it may require refactoring that is not scoped here.
4. **'reducer skips the type'** — assumes the reducer has a skip/ignore mechanism for unknown event types or that adding a new skip is trivial. If the reducer requires an exhaustive match, emitting REVIEW_RESULT without a reducer clause would be a bug, not a no-op.
5. **has_children + complexity fields exist on tickets** — the routing pivot assumes these fields are populated and queryable at claim time.
6. **'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'** — assumes a plan-time graph analysis step already exists or is trivially addable; no design for this computation is given.
findings.suggested_edit: For each of the six assumptions above, add a one-line 'prerequisite check' note in the relevant AC: e.g., 'Verify solidlsp public API covers crash/timeout contract before integrating'; 'Confirm rebar.llm orchestrator extension point is sufficient or note required additions'; 'Audit reducer event-type handling — confirm unknown types are silently ignored or add explicit skip clause'; 'Confirm has_children and complexity are available at claim time'; 'Design or reference existing centrality/blast-radius computation, or descope to a follow-on.'

findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet points) and ## Design summary
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic, and the plan states follow-on work will be filed as separate tickets — but NO child tickets are listed or decomposed within this plan. The acceptance criteria bundle at least 12 distinct deliverable dimensions (Layer-1 deterministic criteria, Layer-2 advisory criteria, criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation, feedback-authoring spec conformance, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event, per-criterion config, bug exemption, experiment-grounded defaults, and docs) into a single epic unit. For a leaf ticket this would be far too large to execute coherently in one session; for an epic it must have children. The plan says children will be filed ('separate linked tickets') but does NOT enumerate them or describe the decomposition strategy — so it is impossible to verify the decomposition is sensible. This is AMBIGUOUS because the plan may have pre-existing children in the tracker that are simply not reproduced here, but as written no child structure is visible.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Child tickets' section enumerating the planned child tickets (one per major AC bucket, e.g., Layer-1 implementation, Layer-2 implementation, registry/orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, config surface, docs), each with a one-line scope statement. This makes decomposition auditable and verifies children cover the parent ACs.

findings.location: ## Design summary — 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'; ## Acceptance Criteria — 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two critical design inputs are externally referenced and not self-contained: (1) the full plan-of-record is in session log '63cc-ec94-4792-464b', and (2) chunking/budget/depth defaults are deferred to 'the experiment results'. An executing agent would need to locate and interpret these external artifacts before acting. If those artifacts are inaccessible or ambiguous, the agent must stop and ask. Additionally, AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' reads as a TBD placeholder — the experiments are future work not yet completed ('see linked experiment work'), meaning concrete defaults are not yet specified at plan-authoring time. The AC for Layer-2 criteria lists 'anti-slop' and 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' without defining what either means, which could require clarification during execution.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the minimum necessary design decisions from the session log directly into the epic description (signing algorithm, material-event definition, default chunk size, budget cap value, depth tiers). For the experiment-dependent defaults, either (a) specify provisional defaults the agent can use and note they will be updated post-experiment, or (b) split the 'set defaults' AC into a separate child ticket that explicitly depends on the experiment results ticket.

findings.location: ## Context — first two paragraphs; ## Design summary — 'posture is to COACH agents to author better plans'

## Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'; 'Attestation … extends the close-gate signing'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan names several specific files/modules it will touch or extend — solidlsp integration, the existing close-gate signing logic, rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points, the `claim` command, and the event reducer — but does NOT enumerate concrete file paths or module names for any of them. It also identifies consumers that must be updated (e.g., the reducer must be taught to skip the new REVIEW_RESULT event type; the `claim` command must gain an attestation-check path; CI integrations may read the attestation) but does not acknowledge blast radius for each. Whether these omissions are problematic depends on whether child tickets will carry the file-level detail, which is unverifiable without the tracker. The plan does explicitly acknowledge fail-open behavior and the sidecar-event-reducer interaction, which partially addresses blast-radius awareness.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' subsection listing at minimum: the claim command module, the close-gate signing module being extended, the reducer (and the skip-type change), the rebar.llm extension points, and the config schema file. Note that child tickets will carry per-file detail. This makes the blast radius legible without requiring codebase access.

findings.location: Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan defers the concrete default values for chunking, budget caps, and depth to experiment results that are 'linked' but not embedded in the plan itself. An executing agent must locate session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` and interpret experiment outputs to know what values to ship as defaults. This is an explicit decision-deferral: if the experiment artifacts are unavailable or ambiguous, the agent faces a real design decision with no fallback guidance in the plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Defaults' sub-section that either (a) states the concrete values derived from experiments (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call, per-plan budget cap = $X, depth = [shallow|standard|deep] thresholds at complexity Y/Z') or (b) defines a fallback policy ('if experiment results are unavailable, use these conservative starting values: ...') so the executor is never blocked.

findings.location: AC list items vs. Design summary
findings.finding: Two potential coherence gaps worth flagging: (1) The Design summary states 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic' and there is an AC line 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on).' However, neither the description nor the AC specifies HOW bug tickets are detected/routed at the gate — the mechanism is absent, making the AC unverifiable without codebase inspection. (2) The AC requires 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern,' but no description section discusses documentation scope, format, or location. This docs requirement is an AC with no corresponding described work. These are borderline; codebase context could resolve the routing question, hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Documentation' section to the plan body calling out what docs artifacts are produced and where they live. For bug-routing, specify the detection mechanism (e.g., ticket type field check) so the AC is verifiable.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (entire list); no 'Testing' or 'Verification' sub-section exists in the plan body
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing-plan section and no AC that describes HOW the gate itself will be tested. There is no mention of: unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (including fail-open/skip paths for unsupported languages), integration tests for the open→in_progress claim flow with/without a valid attestation, regression tests for the coaching-feedback authoring spec, tests for attestation invalidation on material edits, or tests for the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event structure. For a gate that fires on every ticket claim across a broad client base, the absence of any testing narrative is a significant gap — an executor has no guidance on what to test, at what level, or in what order.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section covering at minimum: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion with a fail-open fixture (unsupported lang → skipped = PASS), (2) integration test for claim blocking with missing vs. valid vs. stale attestation, (3) a contract/snapshot test for the `REVIEW_RESULT` event schema, (4) a test confirming material-edit invalidation triggers re-review, and (5) a smoke test that the Layer-2 advisory output conforms to the coaching spec (grounded, severity-ranked, checklist-form).

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps)'; 'scc + lizard (size)'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan makes several assertions about existing infrastructure that an executor must verify against the live codebase before relying on: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumed present and functional for all target languages; (2) 'ast-grep' — assumed installable/available; (3) 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — the orchestrator is described as a reuse of existing extension points, but their current API surface and suitability for per-criterion declarative descriptors is unverified in the plan; (4) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is factored out and extensible; (5) 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR' — assumes the event reducer has a plugin/ignore mechanism. Any of these being absent or different from described would require rework of the approach.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Pre-implementation verification checklist' noting each assumption (solidlsp coverage, ast-grep availability, rebar.llm API surface, close-gate signing factorability, reducer ignore mechanism) that the executor should confirm before starting, with a fallback note for each.

findings.location: Context section: 'rebar has a completion-verifier close gate... when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound'; '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'

Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'; 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'; AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding:
Three potential NIH / over-engineering concerns surface from the plan text alone, though all three require codebase confirmation before a hard FAIL is warranted:

1. **Criteria registry + generic orchestrator**: The plan says 'reuse rebar.llm extension points', implying the runner infrastructure already exists. If the catalog/contracts/prompt/runner already generalises across criteria, adding a *second* declarative registry for plan-review criteria on top of it is a Rule-of-Three risk (one call-site for this specialisation). However, if rebar.llm's registry is not criteria-typed in the way plan-review needs, a thin adapter is justified. Cannot confirm without the live codebase.

2. **Five separate polyglot tooling integrations** (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) each with their own failure-handling wrappers, LLM fallback chains, and INDETERMINATE pathways: this is a meaningful new integration surface. If any of these are already integrated into rebar for other features (e.g. solidlsp is described as 'already in repo'), duplicating their wrappers would be NIH. The plan does not describe reuse of existing wrappers.

3. **Configuration proliferation**: The plan adds at least five new project-level knobs (chunking, budget caps, depth, per-criterion blocking, INDETERMINATE posture). Shipping five config dimensions at once with 'sensible defaults grounded in experiment results' is reasonable only if the experiment work is truly complete; the AC defers this to 'linked experiment work'. If the experiments are not yet done, shipping all five knobs up-front is premature generalisation.
findings.suggested_edit:
For (1): Explicitly state whether the plan-review criteria registry IS the rebar.llm catalog extended, or a parallel structure. If parallel, justify why the existing catalog cannot be extended in-place.

For (2): Add a note to the AC or design summary calling out which tool wrappers already exist in the repo and can be reused vs. which are net-new, so the implementer does not duplicate existing integration code.

For (3): Consider gating the experiment-dependent defaults behind a 'TBD / set at AC sign-off time' note, and explicitly listing which of the five config knobs are required for launch vs. which can ship with hard-coded defaults initially.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all 12 ACs; Success Criteria — all 6 SCs; Follow-on list
findings.finding:
The plan contains **no testing plan at all**. There is no AC, SC, or design section that describes how the gate itself will be tested before shipping. Specifically missing:

- No unit-test or integration-test strategy for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g. 'given a ticket with no `## Acceptance Criteria` section, Layer-1 blocks; given an unsupported language, Layer-1 passes').
- No test strategy for Layer-2 advisory criteria (e.g. fixture tickets used as golden inputs with expected per-criterion verdicts).
- No failure-path coverage for the fail-open polyglot paths (tool crash, timeout, unsupported language → skipped = PASS).
- No test coverage for attestation signing, invalidation-on-material-edit, and staleness detection.
- No test for the `claim` fast-check path (valid attestation passes, absent attestation blocks, `--force` override records correctly).
- No RED-before-GREEN ordering mentioned anywhere.
- No mention of the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event being validated (schema, reducer-skip, fingerprint uniqueness).

The plan is operationally complete in *what* to build but entirely silent on *how correctness will be verified*, which means an executor must invent the test strategy from scratch — a major rework risk.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a dedicated **Testing Plan** section (or expand the ACs) covering at minimum:
- Fixture-based unit tests for each Layer-1 check (one passing and one failing fixture per criterion, including the fail-open unsupported-language case).
- Golden-input integration tests for Layer-2: a small set of fixture tickets with known expected coaching output structure (not exact LLM text, but correct criterion IDs, verdict types, grounding fields populated).
- Contract tests for attestation: sign → verify round-trip; material-edit → invalidation; non-material-edit (tag/comment) → attestation survives.
- Smoke test for `claim` fast-check: (a) no attestation → blocked; (b) stale attestation → blocked; (c) valid attestation → proceeds; (d) `--force` → override event recorded.
- Reducer skip test: a `REVIEW_RESULT` event fed through the reducer must leave hot-path state unchanged.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution: … Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data; per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding:
The plan's end-state reachability has one gap: **the experiment results that ground the chunking/budget/depth defaults are not yet produced or referenced by any concrete deliverable in this epic**. The AC says 'see linked experiment work', but no linked experiment ticket is listed in the plan text, and the Follow-on list does not include experiment execution. If the experiments are a prerequisite, they must be done before this epic's defaults can be set — but the epic treats them as already available. This creates an ambiguous termination condition: the AC for 'defaults grounded in experiment results' cannot be signed off if the experiments are still outstanding.

Separately, the INDETERMINATE outcome's non-blocking default is described in the design summary and partially in the AC ('non-blocking default, configurable'), but there is no explicit acceptance test that shows what observable state 'INDETERMINATE, non-blocking' produces for a downstream CI consumer — only that it exists. This is minor but makes the CI-check AC (#6 SC) harder to verify.
findings.suggested_edit:
Either: (a) Add a prerequisite note naming the experiment ticket(s) and stating that their outputs are an input to this epic's defaults AC; or (b) Change the AC to 'defaults set to conservative hard-coded values at launch; experiment-grounded tuning filed as a follow-on', removing the dependency on unfinished work.

For INDETERMINATE: Add one concrete observable outcome to the AC, e.g. 'a claim on a ticket whose review returned INDETERMINATE proceeds normally and the `REVIEW_RESULT` event records outcome=INDETERMINATE with the budget/tool reason'.

findings.location: Design summary — (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; (2) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; (3) 'extends the close-gate signing'; (4) 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR … reducer skips the type'
findings.finding:
The plan makes at least four load-bearing assertions about the existing codebase that an executor must verify before proceeding. None are flagged as assumptions to confirm:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — the plan treats solidlsp as an existing, reusable integration. If it is only partially integrated (e.g. installed as a dep but not wrapped with a typed caller interface), the Layer-1 file/symbol resolution work is substantially larger than implied.

2. **'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — the plan architects the criteria registry on top of existing rebar.llm extension points. If those extension points don't already expose a per-criterion verdict contract (PASS/AMBIGUOUS/FAIL with location/finding/severity fields), the orchestrator must build that contract from scratch, not merely reuse it.

3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — the attestation design is described as an extension of existing close-gate signing infrastructure. If the close-gate signing is not modular / not designed for reuse from a different trigger point, 'extending' it may require significant refactoring rather than a thin extension.

4. **'reducer skips the type' (REVIEW_RESULT sidecar)** — this implies either the reducer already has a skip-list mechanism, or adding one is trivial. If the reducer is a closed match/dispatch with no skip-list, this requires a new reducer modification that could touch hot-path logic, contradicting the 'out of hot paths' claim.

All four are only confirmable against the live codebase; none can be assessed as wrong from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit:
Add a **Prerequisite verification checklist** section to the plan (or prefix the relevant ACs) that explicitly names each assumption and requires the executor to confirm it before beginning that sub-task. For example:
- '[ ] Confirm solidlsp has a typed caller interface and is not merely installed as a dependency.'
- '[ ] Confirm rebar.llm's runner exposes a per-criterion verdict contract with location/finding/severity fields.'
- '[ ] Confirm close-gate signing is importable/extendable from the open→in_progress trigger path without refactor.'
- '[ ] Confirm the reducer has a skip-list or equivalent mechanism for event types, or scope the work to add one.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: The plan defers a concrete design decision onto the executor: the default values for chunking granularity, budget caps, and review depth are described as 'to be grounded in experiment results' and 'set empirically via experiments', but no experiment results are provided in the plan, and the linked session log is not accessible to a future executor. This is an explicit decision-deferral ('see linked experiment work') that pushes real configuration decisions — ones that directly affect gate behavior — onto the executor. Similarly, the 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality' routing logic has no specified thresholds or decision boundaries, leaving the executor to 'choose appropriate' cutoffs.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline the specific default values (e.g. criteria-per-LLM-call chunk size, per-plan token budget cap, depth tiers by complexity score range, centrality threshold for escalation) derived from the experiments, or (b) explicitly scope a spike sub-task to produce those values before implementation begins, and block implementation on that result. At minimum, name the experiment ticket/log section so the executor can locate the numbers without ambiguity.

findings.location: AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape (standardized `## Acceptance Criteria` across all types), file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff.' vs. Design summary layer-1 description
findings.finding: Two minor coherence gaps exist. (1) The AC bullet for Layer-1 lists 'AC/DD quality' as a Layer-1 deterministic check, but the design body describes AC/DD quality as part of the advisory/coaching posture (Layer 2 criterion 'anti-slop', 'measurability/agent-completable'). The boundary between which quality signals are deterministic/structural vs. advisory is blurred, and an executor cannot tell whether to implement these as blocking deterministic checks or advisory ones. (2) The AC mentions 'destructive sniff' but this term does not appear in the design summary body — there is no description of what constitutes a 'destructive' operation, what the sniff checks for, or what policy applies on detection, making it an orphan AC item. Additionally, 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' appears in both the design summary and the escalation AC but is never defined or described in either place.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence in the design body defining 'destructive sniff' (e.g. 'detects tickets whose file_impact set contains irreversible operations such as schema drops or data migrations'). Clarify whether 'AC/DD quality' belongs in Layer-1 (blocking, deterministic) or Layer-2 (advisory) and remove it from whichever layer it does not belong to. Define 'centrality/blast-radius' or cross-reference a spec for it.

findings.location: All acceptance criteria sections; no dedicated testing section exists in the plan
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing strategy for the gate itself. There is no description of how the plan-review verification gate will be tested: no mention of unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks, no integration tests for the open→in_progress claim path, no test cases for the attestation signing/invalidation logic, no failure-path coverage (e.g. what happens when the LSP server crashes mid-review, when syft times out, when a chunk exhausts its budget), and no mention of red-before-green ordering. For a gate that is explicitly 'fail-open on unsupported stacks', the test plan for the fail-open paths is especially critical and entirely absent. The only vague testing signal is the reference to 'experiment results' for defaults, which is calibration data, not behavioral test coverage.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section (or AC bullet) that specifies: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check including the fail-open/skip path for unsupported languages; (2) integration tests for the claim fast-path (valid cert, stale cert, missing cert, --force override); (3) tests for attestation invalidation on material edits; (4) budget-exhaustion → INDETERMINATE path test; (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema validation. Note which tests should run red before implementation begins.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers)'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan makes several unverified assertions about the existing codebase that an executor must confirm before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — the plan assumes solidlsp is available and integrated; if it is not at the expected integration point, the Layer-1 file-resolution checks need to be built from scratch. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)' — the plan assumes these extension points exist, are stable, and are sufficient for the criteria orchestrator; if the contracts don't support per-criterion declarative descriptors natively, significant adapter work is needed. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is factored in a reusable way. These are resolvable against the live codebase but cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Confirmed dependencies' note (or a spike task) that explicitly verifies: solidlsp integration point and API surface, rebar.llm extension point contracts that will be reused, and the close-gate signing module's reuse surface. This de-risks the plan before execution begins.

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; 'a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE (many languages, frameworks...)'
Title ('Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)'), Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points), Follow-on work section (F1–F12)
findings.finding: This ticket is framed as an epic, and the plan gestures at decomposition (Layer 1 / Layer 2, a dozen distinct ACs, 12 named follow-ons), but no child tickets are present or referenced as already filed. The 12 acceptance criteria span at least 6–8 independently shippable sub-systems: (1) deterministic Layer-1 criteria + polyglot tooling, (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching spec, (3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation + signing + invalidation, (5) claim integration, (6) configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, (8) experiment-grounded defaults + docs. Each of these is a non-trivial engineering slice. Without filed children, it is unclear whether this epic is meant to be executed as a monolithic leaf by a single agent session, or whether child decomposition is implicitly expected before execution begins. If it is a leaf, it is far too large to execute coherently in one session. If it is an epic-with-children, the children are absent. The Follow-on list (F1–F12) names future work but explicitly excludes it from scope, reinforcing that all 12 ACs must be delivered here — a scope that is very large. Codebase access would be needed to confirm whether child tickets exist in the tracker.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) file child tickets covering each of the 8 sub-systems above and update this epic's description to reference them (turning it into a true parent with no direct implementation obligation), or (b) if it truly is a leaf, narrow the ACs to a single vertical slice (e.g., 'Layer-1 structural floor + claim integration + attestation') and defer Layer 2, observability, docs, and experiment-grounding to follow-on tickets.

findings.location: Multiple AC bullets; Design summary ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'); Design summary ('Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b')
findings.finding:
Several elements require an executing agent to make unspecified design decisions or to locate off-plan information:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults (AC bullet 11):** 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — the experiments are referenced but not described in the plan, and the session log is cited by an opaque ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') without a URL or inline summary. An agent cannot retrieve these results without external access that may not be available, making this a real TBD.

2. **Criteria registry schema:** The plan says 'each criterion is a declarative descriptor' and instructs the agent to 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points', but it does not specify what those extension points look like or where they live. The agent must discover this from the codebase — legitimate, but the AC should at least name the file/interface.

3. **REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema:** The AC mentions 'finding fingerprints + metadata + override justifications' but does not define the event schema fields, fingerprint algorithm, or how 'reducer-ignored' is implemented in rebar's event store. An agent would have to infer or invent these.

4. **'Triggered overlays (router-selected)':** AC Layer-2 bullet names 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' but neither the criteria nor the routing logic for activating them is specified anywhere in the plan text.

5. **Centrality/blast-radius computation:** 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — no definition of the metric, algorithm, or data source is given.

Items 1 and 3–5 are genuine decision-deferrals that will force an executing agent to stop and ask or make arbitrary choices.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision: (1) inline the key experiment-result numbers (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria, budget cap = M tokens, depth thresholds = [X, Y]') or mark them as a spike sub-task; (2) name the file/class that exposes rebar.llm's extension points; (3) add a minimal REVIEW_RESULT schema table (event_type, fields, fingerprint = SHA-256 of criterion_id+finding_text+location); (4) define what 'triggered overlay' means and list the router conditions; (5) define centrality (e.g., 'betweenness centrality in the file-import graph, computed by ast-grep call graph').

findings.location: Context section ('~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'); Design summary ('posture is to COACH agents to author better plans'); AC bullet on coaching spec; Follow-on F1

AC bullet 1 ('Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep) … syft + registry + fallback … scc + lizard'); AC bullet 4 ('Attestation … extends the close-gate signing'); AC bullet 5 ('claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'); AC bullet 7 ('REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event … reducer skips the type')
findings.finding:
The plan names several external modules and integration points but does not provide file paths, class names, or module coordinates for any of them, making it impossible to confirm without codebase access:

1. **'solidlsp (already in repo)'** — no path given; the agent must search for it.
2. **'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'** — no file/module path; the agent must discover the extension interface.
3. **'extends the close-gate signing'** — the close-gate signing code is not located; the agent must find and modify it without guidance on where it lives.
4. **'claim integration'** — the claim command/handler is not located; blast radius of touching it (other commands that also call claim? tests? CI hooks?) is unacknowledged.
5. **'reducer skips the type'** — the event reducer is not located; a change there could affect all event replay; no mention of existing reducer tests or consumers of the event stream.
6. **syft, scc, lizard, ast-grep** — invocation interfaces (subprocess? library?) and version pins are unspecified.

The plan does acknowledge fail-open behavior and the broad scope, but it does not name any affected files or acknowledge the blast radius of modifying core infrastructure like the event reducer and the claim command. These are high-blast-radius touch-points that warrant explicit acknowledgement per G1G2.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules to touch' table listing: (a) the solidlsp module path, (b) the rebar.llm extension entry point file, (c) the close-gate signing module, (d) the claim command handler file, (e) the event reducer file, (f) any existing test suites covering (d) and (e). Acknowledge that modifying the event reducer and the claim command are cross-cutting changes and that all callers of claim must be regression-tested.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all 12 bullet points; 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate'
findings.finding: This is a very large epic with at least 12 distinct acceptance criteria spanning: a deterministic Layer-1 checker (with ~8 sub-checks across polyglot tooling integrations), a Layer-2 advisory engine (~13 named criteria plus triggered overlays), a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, attestation + signing + invalidation logic, claim-gate integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget, coaching feedback authoring, a new REVIEW_RESULT event type + reducer skip, per-criterion config knob, bug-exemption routing, experiment-grounded defaults, and documentation. The plan acknowledges 'too big to one-shot' as a failure mode it is trying to prevent, yet presents itself as a single unit of work. There is no decomposition into child tickets (the Follow-on work section explicitly defers 12 items but does NOT decompose the epic itself into executable slices). For a leaf ticket this is far too large; as a parent/epic it lacks child-ticket decomposition. An agent attempting to execute this in one session would face exactly the compound-error risk the plan describes.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose the epic into at minimum these vertical-slice child tickets: (1) Attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation; (2) claim-gate fast-check integration + --force override; (3) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + fingerprint schema; (4) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator scaffold; (5) Layer-1 deterministic criteria (can be further split by tool: LSP/ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard, AC-shape, DAG); (6) Layer-2 advisory criteria (split by criterion group); (7) Configurable chunking/routing/budget/escalation; (8) Coaching feedback-authoring conformance; (9) Experiment-grounded defaults; (10) Documentation. Each child should be independently executable and verifiable.

findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1; Acceptance Criteria bullets on Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim integration; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: The plan references 'experiment results' and 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' as the grounding for defaults, but those artifacts are external and not reproduced. An agent executing this ticket cannot independently verify that the end-state (specifically AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results') is reachable without access to those logs. Additionally, the Layer-2 'bounded broad open-ended pass' and 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' criteria have no termination condition specified — it is unclear when the overlay set is 'complete'. The union of described steps does plausibly reach most stated ACs, but these two gaps create uncertainty about whether the agent knows when it is done.
findings.suggested_edit: Either reproduce the relevant experiment conclusions inline (or in a linked, agent-accessible doc), or rewrite that AC as 'Defaults documented with rationale; empirical validation is a follow-on.' For triggered overlays, enumerate the overlay triggers explicitly or reference a bounded list so the agent has a clear done-state.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep ... syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)'; 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan names five distinct external tools (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) plus an LLM fallback for a set of checks that are individually narrow (e.g., oversize = line count). Without codebase access it cannot be confirmed whether lighter-weight alternatives already exist in rebar, or whether the criteria registry/orchestrator duplicates an existing rebar.llm catalog pattern. The plan explicitly claims reuse ('reusing rebar.llm extension points') which is the right instinct, but the five-tool surface is large and each adds an operational dependency. The Rule-of-Three concern is mild here because the tools serve distinct check families, but the cumulative integration surface is worth scrutinizing.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit statement in the plan confirming which of the five tools are already integrated (vs. net-new dependencies) and why a simpler fallback (e.g., raw file line count for scc, grep for lizard) is insufficient. This removes ambiguity about NIH risk.

findings.location: Design summary ('LAYER 1 ... BLOCKS') vs. Acceptance Criteria ('judgment criteria are advisory by default') vs. Success Criteria bullet 2 ('deterministic Layer-1 floor blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks'); Design summary ('INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default') vs. Acceptance Criteria ('per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override')
findings.finding: Two minor internal tensions: (1) The design says Layer-1 'BLOCKS' categorically, but the AC says 'fail-open on any unsupported language/tool (skipped = pass)' — this is reconcilable but the design summary omits the fail-open carve-out for Layer-1, making it sound absolute when it is not. (2) The design summary states 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)' but the per-criterion config knob (advisory | blocking-with-override) does not mention INDETERMINATE as a third state — it is unclear whether INDETERMINATE can be made blocking via that knob. These are not showstoppers but an agent implementing both could produce inconsistent behavior.
findings.suggested_edit: In the Design summary, revise the Layer-1 description to read 'BLOCKS on resolved checks; skips (= pass) on unsupported lang/tool/crash/timeout.' For the per-criterion config, explicitly note whether INDETERMINATE is a distinct outcome from AMBIGUOUS/FAIL and whether it is separately configurable.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python ... file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)'; Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan does not name the specific files or modules to be created/modified, nor does it identify the existing close-gate signing module that is to be extended. The phrase 'extends the close-gate signing' implies a specific existing code path, but without codebase access or file names it cannot be confirmed that this is accurate or that the blast radius (consumers of the claim flow, the reducer, the event schema) has been fully enumerated. The plan acknowledges blast radius at a high level ('material event commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit') but does not enumerate which existing event consumers/reducers must be updated to skip REVIEW_RESULT. This is AMBIGUOUS rather than a clear failure because the plan is an epic-level description, not a sprint task.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' section listing at minimum: the claim handler, the event schema module, the reducer(s) that must skip REVIEW_RESULT, and the close-gate signing module being extended. This gives the agent concrete anchors and surfaces any missed consumers.

findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 ('Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'); Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; AC: 'Docs: how the gate works…'
findings.finding: Three acceptance criteria are not verifiable within a single agent session: (1) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably' requires a ground-truth comparison across multiple plan-revision cycles, which cannot be demonstrated in-session; it is an outcome metric, not a deliverable criterion. (2) 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results' — the experiments are referenced externally and an agent cannot confirm this criterion is met without access to those results. (3) 'Docs: how the gate works…' is broadly scoped but verifiable if interpreted as 'documentation file exists and covers listed topics'; however, it borders on unmeasurable without a reviewer checklist. The first criterion is the most problematic: 'measurably help' is a real-user/multi-session validation requirement.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a concrete, in-session-checkable criterion such as: 'Each advisory finding includes: criterion_id, quoted location, specific finding text, severity, and a suggested_edit field; a test fixture with a known-flawed plan produces at least N findings with all fields populated.' For the experiment-defaults AC, either inline the conclusions or reframe as 'defaults are documented with stated rationale.'

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: The plan appropriately calls for reusing existing rebar.llm extension points (catalog, contracts, prompt, runner) rather than building from scratch, which is good. However, several aspects warrant an NIH/over-engineering check that requires codebase access to resolve: (1) The plan introduces a new 'criteria registry' with declarative descriptors — it is unclear whether rebar already has a generic criterion/check registry pattern (e.g., in the completion-verifier) that could be extended rather than creating a new one. (2) The dual-layer model (Layer-1 deterministic + Layer-2 advisory) with per-criterion config knobs (advisory | blocking-with-override), routing logic, chunk-size knobs, escalation-by-centrality, and a sidecar event type adds substantial configuration surface. Whether this is justified or over-engineered depends on what the existing completion-verifier already implements and exposes. (3) The 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event' pattern — if the completion-verifier already has a comparable sidecar event, a new type may be gratuitous; if not, it is well-motivated.
findings.suggested_edit: Before finalizing, explicitly compare each new registry/event/config surface against what the completion-verifier already provides and document which are genuinely novel vs. reuses. Collapse anything that is a one-call-site abstraction into the call site.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: This AC references 'the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' as the basis for defaults, but no experiment tickets or results are linked in the plan. This creates an out-of-session dependency: the agent implementing this AC cannot determine the correct defaults without access to external experiment data that is not provided in the plan. Similarly, the Success Criteria item 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' contains the word 'measurably' without defining the metric or threshold, making it unverifiable within the session. The AC 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern' is documentation completeness, which is broad and lacks a concrete checklist of what 'done' looks like for docs.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Either inline the experiment-derived defaults (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call, budget cap = X tokens') or explicitly state placeholder defaults that can be tuned later, removing the dependency on unlinked experiment results. 2. Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a concrete proxy (e.g., 'each advisory finding includes a criterion reference, a file/section location quote, and a suggested edit'). 3. Add a specific list of doc sections required.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override' and 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record (extends the close-gate signing)'
findings.finding: The plan describes a two-phase execution model (out-of-band heavy review + fast claim-time check) and a signing/invalidation scheme, but does not specify a concrete verification procedure an agent or reviewer can execute to confirm the gate is working end-to-end. Specifically: (1) There is no described test scenario or integration test that exercises the full open→in_progress flow with a stale attestation (should block), a valid attestation (should pass), and a --force override (should record). (2) 'Material event commit binding' is described conceptually but the exact events that constitute 'material' vs. 'immaterial' (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits vs. tags/comments/links/assignee) are listed in the design summary but not carried into any AC, making it ambiguous whether the implementing agent would produce the right invalidation logic. (3) Whether rebar's existing event model supports 'reducer-ignored sidecar events' without changes to the reducer cannot be confirmed without codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC explicitly enumerating the integration-test scenarios for the gate: (a) claim with no attestation → blocked, (b) claim with valid attestation → allowed, (c) claim after material edit → blocked, (d) claim with --force → allowed + override logged. Also promote the material-vs-immaterial event list from the design summary into a binding AC.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation' and 'Attestation: ... extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan names no specific files, modules, classes, or symbols that will be created or modified. For a feature of this scope — integrating into the open→in_progress transition, extending the close-gate attestation/signing infrastructure, adding a sidecar event type to the reducer, wiring in solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard, and adding the criteria orchestrator — the blast radius across the existing codebase is likely large. Without named files or modules (e.g., 'modify src/rebar/claim.py', 'extend src/rebar/attestation.py', 'add src/rebar/plan_review/'), it is impossible to confirm plausibility or identify missing consumers. This is particularly important for: (a) the reducer that must be updated to skip REVIEW_RESULT events, (b) the claim command that must check the attestation, and (c) the signing infrastructure shared with the close gate.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules affected' section listing at minimum: the claim entrypoint file, the attestation/signing module, the reducer (to add the skip), the new plan_review package root, and any config schema files. Even approximate names (based on the existing close-gate structure) would let a reviewer confirm plausibility.

findings.location: Multiple sections
findings.finding: Two internal tensions exist: (1) The design summary states 'LAYER 1 … BLOCKS' and 'LAYER 2 … SURFACES findings … and never blocks by default,' but the AC states 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override' and 'A project can configure risk posture … and per-criterion blocking via config.' This is mostly coherent, but the design summary's phrasing 'Sound checks block; judgment is advisory; AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' implies AMBIGUOUS is a third outcome that escalates — yet the AC and Success Criteria never mention an 'adjudicator' or escalation path as something that must be implemented, only that INDETERMINATE is 'non-blocking by default, configurable.' The handling of AMBIGUOUS (escalate) vs. INDETERMINATE (non-blocking) is conflated in the ACs. (2) The Success Criteria says 'Claiming a ticket … requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation; absence or a material-edit-invalidated signature blocks the claim, with a recorded --force override available' — but the design summary says the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND. If the review is out-of-band, there must be an initial state where no attestation exists but work can still start; the plan does not clarify the bootstrap/first-run behavior (does an agent without any attestation get immediately blocked forever?).
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add a dedicated AC clarifying the AMBIGUOUS-verdict escalation path: is it implemented in this epic or deferred? 2. Add a bootstrap AC: on first run or when no prior attestation exists, the agent must run the review to generate one before claiming — document this flow explicitly so it is not confused with 'absence blocks' (which would create a deadlock if the review tool is unavailable).

findings.location: Entire plan — epic with no child tickets listed; Acceptance Criteria section (12 distinct ACs)
findings.finding: This is explicitly labeled an 'epic' and the plan acknowledges a large scope: two full implementation layers (Layer-1 with ~8 deterministic criteria, Layer-2 with ~13 advisory criteria), a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, attestation/signing infrastructure, claim integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation, a sidecar event system, per-criterion config, documentation, and experiment-grounded defaults. The plan lists 12 acceptance criteria, references an experiment program whose results must be produced, and lists 12 follow-on items — all signals of very large scope. Yet no child tickets are listed or referenced. The plan states 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity' and the gate itself routes based on has_children — but this epic has no children. This is an internal irony and a practical problem: an agent claiming this ticket would face a single session encompassing months of work.
