findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either inline the decision in the plan or explicitly scope it out: (1) Replace 'grounded in experiment results' with concrete placeholder defaults (e.g., 'default chunk size: 3 criteria/call; per-plan budget cap: $X; depth levels: shallow/standard/deep at complexity thresholds T1/T2') and note they may be tuned post-experiment. (2) Inline the key design decisions from session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` that are prerequisites for implementation; do not rely on the log as the spec. (3) Define the centrality/blast-radius computation approach (e.g., 'file-level reverse-dependency graph from LSP references; centrality = in-degree count'). (4) Define 'anti-slop' operationally (e.g., 'checks for filler phrases, vague scope, or zero actionable steps'). (5) List at least the known triggered overlays and their router conditions. (6) Describe the 2-step blind-restate procedure explicitly.

findings.location: Design summary ('Two layers') vs. AC ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)')
findings.finding: The plan states that chunking/budget/depth defaults are to be 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work),' but the experiment work is not itself part of this epic and the linked session log is external to the plan text. It is therefore ambiguous whether the experiments have been completed and their results are available to inform the defaults, or whether defaults must be chosen before the experiments are done. Relatedly, the AC lists 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' as an acceptance criterion of THIS ticket, creating a potential circular dependency with a deliverable not explicitly scoped in. No outright contradiction, but the coupling is unresolved.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) explicitly state that the experiment results pre-exist in the session log and cite the specific conclusions, or (b) split the 'defaults from experiments' AC into a follow-on so this epic can ship with provisional defaults and the experiment ticket closes the loop.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Execution' bullet; AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)'
findings.finding: Multiple design decisions are deferred or left underspecified, requiring an executor to make real design choices without guidance: (1) 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB' — no default value, no range, no algorithm for choosing it empirically. (2) 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — the experiments are not in scope; the executor must decide what to do when results are absent. (3) 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — 'stronger model' is undefined (which models? what threshold triggers escalation?). (4) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — the algorithm or tool for centrality computation is not named. (5) 'generous initial budget' — no numeric guidance given. (6) 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' — what triggers them and what they contain is unspecified. Each of these forces an executor to make substantive architecture decisions mid-implementation.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either: (a) provide a concrete default (e.g. 'default chunk size = 3 criteria per call; override via config key `plan_review.chunk_size`'), or (b) explicitly move it to a follow-on ticket with a placeholder constant in the code so the executor isn't blocked. Name the specific models in the escalation ladder or say 'same model-alias mechanism as the completion-verifier.'

findings.location: Title vs. full plan body
Entire plan; AC section (12 distinct top-level ACs covering infra, two layers, registry, attestation, claim integration, config, feedback spec, observability, docs, and experiment-grounded defaults)
findings.finding: This is an epic explicitly self-described as such, and it acknowledges 12 follow-on work items — yet it is structured as a single executable ticket rather than a parent with child tickets. The AC list spans at minimum 8 distinct deliverable clusters: (1) Layer-1 deterministic checks, (2) Layer-2 advisory checks, (3) criteria registry + orchestrator, (4) attestation signing, (5) `claim` integration, (6) config/routing/escalation/budget, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer, (8) docs. Each of these is a meaningful implementation unit. Executing all in one session is not feasible; the plan's own Design summary acknowledges the work is large enough to require a chunking/budget/checkpointing subsystem. There are no child tickets defined, no decomposition into subtasks, and no sequencing ordering the dependencies among the eight clusters.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets, at minimum: (C1) Layer-1 structural checks + fail-open tooling harness, (C2) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching feedback spec, (C3) Criteria registry + orchestrator, (C4) Attestation + material-event invalidation, (C5) `claim` gate integration, (C6) Config surface + routing/escalation/budget, (C7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer skip, (C8) Docs. Keep this ticket as the parent/epic with the design summary and cross-cutting ACs; each child gets its own scoped ACs.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling' bullet ('solidlsp (already in repo)'); 'Attestation' bullet ('extends the close-gate signing'); 'Criteria registry' bullet ('reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner')
findings.finding: The plan makes at least three factual assertions about existing infrastructure that an executor must verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — if absent or on a different interface, Layer-1 file-resolution breaks. (2) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes close-gate signing exists and has an extension point; if the signing API isn't designed for reuse the approach may need rework. (3) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points are stable and generic enough; if they're tightly coupled to completion-verification they may not compose cleanly. None of these can be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified pre-conditions' section listing each assumption with the specific interface/module/function name that has already been confirmed to exist, so an executor can do a quick grep/import check rather than a design investigation.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: … anti-slop; plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass'
findings.finding: Two ACs are not verifiable within a single working session: (1) 'Defaults grounded in experiment results' requires external experiment data that is not in-scope for this ticket — an agent cannot complete this AC without results from work that hasn't been filed yet. (2) 'Layer-2 advisory criteria … measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria #3) — 'measurably help' implies empirical validation across agent runs, which is out-of-session validation. Additionally, the docs AC ('how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern') is broadly stated; it is completable but would benefit from specifying the deliverable format (e.g., 'a markdown file at docs/plan-review-gate.md').
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Replace 'Defaults grounded in experiment results' with 'Ships with provisional defaults (chunk_size=N, budget=M tokens); defaults are updated by a follow-on experiment ticket.' (2) Change 'measurably help' to 'each finding includes criterion_id, location quote, severity, and suggested_edit fields, conforming to the coaching spec.' (3) Anchor the docs AC to a specific output path.

findings.location: AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'; Design summary: 'Attestation … Bound to the latest MATERIAL event commit'
findings.finding: The plan names `claim` as the integration point and references 'close-gate signing' as the pattern to extend, but does not name any specific files, modules, or symbols to be modified. For a feature of this blast radius (touching the ticket state-machine's open→in_progress path, the event store's reducer, the attestation signing module, and the LLM runner), the absence of even approximate file targets makes it impossible to assess blast radius or identify consumers that might break. This cannot be resolved from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/modules expected to change' table listing at minimum: the claim handler, the event reducer (with the reducer-skip logic for REVIEW_RESULT), the signing module, and the LLM runner entry point. Flag any that are new vs. modified.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'Polyglot tooling: solidlsp + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'criteria registry' with 'declarative descriptors' and a 'generic orchestrator.' It's plausible this is genuinely new infrastructure, but without codebase access it's unclear whether the completion-verifier already has a criteria/descriptor/runner pattern that could be generalized rather than duplicated. If the close-gate already has a per-criterion runner loop, building a second one risks NIH. Similarly, the toolchain (solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard) is a large new dependency surface — it cannot be confirmed from plan text whether all five tools are necessary or whether existing repo tooling covers some of the same ground.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Reuse vs. new' note: for each new component (registry, orchestrator, tooling harness), state whether it is net-new or a generalization of an existing component, and why generalization was not feasible if net-new.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — entire list; Success Criteria SC3; AC bullet 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented'
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing strategy for the gate itself. There is no mention of: (a) unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., a test that a malformed AC shape triggers a block, a test that an unsupported language skips rather than fails), (b) integration tests for the open→in_progress claim path (e.g., absent attestation blocks; stale attestation after a material edit blocks; --force override succeeds with justification), (c) contract/behavioral tests for the Layer-2 coaching output (e.g., findings are grounded/severity-ranked/checklist-form as the coaching spec requires), (d) failure-path coverage for polyglot tooling (solidlsp crash → skipped/PASS, not FAIL), or (e) RED-before-GREEN ordering. The Docs AC is mentioned but test coverage is absent. Given the plan's own stated risk (~44% agent failures from plan defects), leaving the gate itself untested is a significant gap.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated Testing Plan section (or expand Acceptance Criteria) covering: 1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion (happy path + failure path + unsupported-lang skip); 2) Integration test: claim attempt with missing/stale/valid attestation; 3) Regression snapshot tests: a set of golden ticket descriptions → expected per-criterion verdicts, to guard against coaching-spec drift; 4) Failure-injection tests for each external tool (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) to verify fail-open behavior; 5) A note on RED-before-GREEN ordering (write failing tests for the claim gate before implementing the signing check).

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' bullet; AC bullets: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing… Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'; Success Criteria SC1
findings.finding: Two end-state gaps create ambiguity about when the agent can declare the epic 'done': (1) The plan defers default values for chunking, budget caps, and depth to 'the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — but the experiments are not scoped in this ticket and the session log reference ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') is external. If experiments are a prerequisite, they must be completed before this epic's AC can be checked; if they are follow-on, the AC should say 'best-effort empirical defaults, revisable'. As written, AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' cannot be verified without access to those results. (2) The centrality/blast-radius computation is described as 'computed at plan time' in the design summary and referenced in the escalation AC, but no AC or step explains *how* it is computed or what the acceptance bar is — making that sub-requirement unverifiable. These gaps mean a reviewing agent could reach all stated steps and still be unable to confirm the AC is satisfied.
findings.suggested_edit: For (1): either inline the experiment scope into this ticket or change the AC to 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth are set to [X/Y/Z] as a documented starting point, revisable via follow-on F-tickets' so the criterion is checkable without external artifacts. For (2): add an AC sub-item such as 'Centrality/blast-radius is computed using [method, e.g., reverse-dependency graph from solidlsp call-sites] and the computed value is recorded on the attestation; a test verifies a known high-centrality file receives a higher escalation tier than a leaf file.'

findings.location: ## Design summary, ## Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullet points), ## Success Criteria (all 6 bullets)
findings.finding: This is a large, multi-component epic with 12 distinct acceptance criteria spanning: (1) a deterministic Layer-1 structural checker with ~8 sub-criteria, (2) an advisory Layer-2 reviewer with ~12 sub-criteria plus triggered overlays and a broad pass, (3) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) a signing/attestation system with material-event-commit binding and invalidation logic, (5) `claim` command integration, (6) configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing system, (7) feedback-authoring specification conformance, (8) a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging, (9) per-criterion config knobs, (10) bug-exemption routing, (11) experiment-grounded defaults (requiring prior experiment completion), and (12) documentation. The plan explicitly describes this as an 'epic' but provides no child ticket decomposition — it lists 12 follow-on items (F1–F12) that are post-epic, but the epic itself has no children decomposing its own implementation. Each of the 12 ACs is non-trivial and involves distinct subsystems (LSP tooling, signing, event sourcing, LLM orchestration, config surface, CLI integration). Executing this as a single leaf ticket would require an agent to one-shot all of it coherently, which is not feasible.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose this epic into at least the following child tickets before marking in_progress: (a) Layer-1 deterministic criteria implementation (polyglot tooling integration: solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard; fail-open harness); (b) Layer-2 advisory criteria implementation (LLM-based per-criterion reviewer, coaching feedback spec); (c) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator (declarative descriptors, reusing rebar.llm extension points); (d) Attestation / signing system (material-event-commit binding, invalidation, coverage record); (e) `claim` integration (fast attestation check, --force override); (f) Routing / chunking / escalation / budget / checkpointing system; (g) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip + override logging; (h) Configuration surface + sensible defaults (grounded in experiment results); (i) Documentation. Each child should be scoped to one coherent session of agent work.

findings.location: Multiple sections: Design summary ('default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'), AC bullet 11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'), Design summary ('escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality'), Design summary ('a REVIEW PROCESS, not a dialog')
findings.finding:
Several decision points are explicitly deferred or externalized in ways that would force an executing agent to stop and seek clarification:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults (AC #11):** 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)' — the experiment work is a prerequisite that is not part of this epic and is not linked to a completed artifact. An agent cannot set these defaults without the experiment results, and the plan gives no fallback values or interim placeholder defaults.

2. **Chunking chunk-size:** 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB… default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — same dependency as above; the actual default value is TBD pending experiments.

3. **Escalation model identity:** 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — no model is named, no threshold values are given for size/complexity/centrality that would trigger escalation. An agent must 'choose an appropriate' threshold.

4. **Session log reference:** 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — this is an external artifact not included in the plan. If the agent cannot access it (e.g., it is an opaque session ID), critical design decisions are unavailable.

5. **Triggered overlays (Layer-2):** 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' — no specification of what triggers them or what they contain; an agent would need to decide this independently.

6. **INDETERMINATE configurability:** 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)' with no specification of the config key name, type, or valid values.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either: (a) supply a concrete interim value/spec directly in the plan (e.g., 'default chunk size = 3 criteria per call pending experiments; update after experiment ticket #X closes'), or (b) explicitly make it a child ticket prerequisite that must be completed before this ticket starts. Specifically: (1) Add a concrete fallback default for chunk size (e.g., 3) and budget cap (e.g., 50k tokens) that the experiment results will later tune — document this as 'tunable'; (2) Name the escalation model tier (e.g., 'frontier = GPT-4o or Claude Opus; default = GPT-4o-mini') and provide concrete thresholds (e.g., complexity > 7 OR file_count > 20); (3) Either inline the relevant design decisions from session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b or attach them as a structured appendix; (4) Specify the triggered overlay triggers and their criteria IDs; (5) Name the INDETERMINATE config key (e.g., `plan_review.indeterminate_blocks: false`).

findings.location: Design summary (Trigger/enforcement) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC line: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)')
findings.finding: The plan says defaults will be set 'EMPIRICALLY via experiments' but the experiment work is referenced only as a follow-on assumption ('see linked experiment work') rather than being a stated pre-requisite or child ticket in this epic. If the experiments are not done before this epic ships, the AC requiring empirically-grounded defaults cannot be satisfied — creating a potential internal contradiction between the deliverable scope and the completability of that AC. Additionally, the plan states INDETERMINATE is 'non-blocking by default' in the Design summary, which is consistent with the AC ('non-blocking default, configurable'), so no contradiction there. The tension is specifically around experiment-grounded defaults.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) explicitly list the experiment work as a prerequisite (blocked-by link) with the understanding defaults may be placeholder until experiments complete, or (b) restate the AC as 'Defaults are documented with rationale; empirical tuning is a follow-on', removing the dependency on not-yet-done experiments from this epic's ACs.

findings.location: Design summary (Criteria registry): 'Frozen within a review loop (convergence), grown between versions.' / AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.'
findings.finding: Several key design decisions are deferred without resolution: (1) 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB' — no default chunk size is given in the plan body (only promised to come from experiment results that are a linked/out-of-band dependency); (2) 'router-selected triggered overlays' — no definition of what triggers an overlay or what the overlay set contains; (3) 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — centrality metric is not defined (is it call-graph depth? fan-in count? blast-radius score?); (4) 'MATERIAL event commit' binding is described but the definition of 'material' (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits) is stated at high level — an executor needs to know the exact event fields or commit fields that constitute materiality to implement invalidation correctly. These are real design decisions pushed to the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a section enumerating: (a) a concrete default chunk size (even a placeholder like N=5 pending experiments), (b) the list of named overlay criteria and their trigger conditions, (c) the centrality metric definition (e.g., LSP call-graph in-degree >= threshold T), and (d) the exact set of ticket-event fields/commit fields whose change constitutes a 'material edit'.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance Criteria (12 distinct AC lines spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim integration, configurable routing, feedback authoring, REVIEW_RESULT event, per-criterion config, docs, experiment-grounded defaults)
findings.finding: This epic bundles at minimum 6–7 independently shippable subsystems into a single ticket: (1) Layer-1 deterministic checks with polyglot tooling, (2) Layer-2 advisory LLM-based criteria (11+ named criteria + overlays + broad pass), (3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation signing + invalidation, (5) `claim` integration, (6) configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, (8) docs. The plan explicitly labels this an 'epic' but provides no decomposition into child tickets. The Follow-on section defers 12 items but the in-scope work itself is still enormous. For an epic, the plan should enumerate child tickets and their sequencing; for a leaf, it is far too large to execute coherently in one session. As written, no decomposition into children is present.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets for at minimum: (a) Layer-1 floor implementation, (b) Layer-2 criteria + orchestrator, (c) Attestation + claim integration, (d) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer skip, (e) configurable routing/chunking/budget, (f) docs. Sequence them with dependency links. The epic description can remain as-is but must reference the child ticket IDs.

findings.location: Design summary (Polyglot tooling): 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size).' / 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase that cannot be verified from plan text alone: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumes solidlsp is present, configured, and exposes the interfaces needed for file/symbol resolution; (2) 'reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist and are sufficient for a generic criterion orchestrator without modification; (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate attestation module is factored such that its signing logic is reusable. If any of these assumptions are wrong, the implementation approach changes materially.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified preconditions' section listing: solidlsp interface confirmed at [file/module], rebar.llm extension points confirmed at [file/module], close-gate signing module confirmed reusable at [file/module]. If not yet verified, mark these as assumptions to be validated in the first implementation step.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' / AC: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings (grounded, specific, severity-ranked, checklist-form) that measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Two ACs are not verifiable within a single working session: (1) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably' implies an empirical outcome (before/after revision rates, FP/remediation data) that cannot be confirmed in-session; this is the same data collection deferred to follow-on F1. (2) 'Defaults grounded in experiment results' — the experiment work is out-of-band and not part of this epic's executable scope, making this AC unverifiable at close time. Both of these reference out-of-session validation.
findings.suggested_edit: Rewrite AC (1) as: 'Layer-2 findings conform to the coaching spec (grounded in criterion + location, specific, severity-ranked, checklist-form); conformance is verifiable by automated spec-check or manual rubric.' Rewrite AC (2) as: 'Chunking/budget/depth defaults are documented with stated rationale; empirical calibration is tracked under follow-on F1.'

findings.location: AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override.' / Design summary: 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress.'
findings.finding: The plan names the `claim` command as the integration point and mentions `REVIEW_RESULT` as a new event type and the reducer must skip it — but it does not name the specific files, modules, or symbols that will be modified: the claim handler module, the reducer event-type dispatch table, the event schema definitions, or the attestation storage layer. For a change touching the rebar core event/state machine (reducer), the blast radius could be significant. Without naming these targets it is impossible to confirm whether the blast radius is acknowledged.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/modules to be modified' list naming at minimum: the claim command handler, the reducer dispatch (to add REVIEW_RESULT skip), the event schema module, and the attestation signing module. Note any consumers of the claim flow that will need updating (e.g., CI integration, status-change hooks).

findings.location: Design summary (Criteria registry): 'each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner).' / 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new 'criteria registry' with a 'generic orchestrator' and a new sidecar event type. The claim that this reuses 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' is plausible if those extension points are genuinely generic, but without codebase access it is not possible to confirm whether a simpler, direct implementation would suffice or whether the generic orchestrator adds a layer used at only one call-site (Rule-of-Three concern). Similarly, the 'declarative descriptor' registry pattern adds abstraction; if there are only ~15 fixed criteria and no plugin/external-contributor requirement in scope, a simpler direct dispatch could serve. Cannot confirm or deny without seeing the existing rebar.llm structure.
findings.suggested_edit: In the design summary, briefly justify why the declarative-registry + generic-orchestrator approach is warranted over direct dispatch (e.g., 'enables per-criterion config knob, versioning, and future project-supplied criteria per F8 without code changes').

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' bullet: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers)'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'solidlsp (already in repo)' and 'ast-grep' as available dependencies, and that 'rebar.llm extension points' exist for the orchestrator to reuse. These are load-bearing claims: if solidlsp is absent, differently scoped, or not importable from the new gate code, the Layer-1 file/symbol resolution criterion cannot be built as described. Similarly, the claim that rebar.llm already exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner interfaces reusable verbatim is unverified from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a concrete verification step in the plan (or in a pre-work AC) that confirms: (1) solidlsp is importable and its LSP client API matches the intended usage, (2) ast-grep is installed/available in the target execution environment, and (3) the rebar.llm extension points (catalog, prompt, runner) actually expose the interfaces the orchestrator will rely on. Reference the specific modules/classes by name.

findings.location: Context section, paragraph 1
Acceptance Criteria item 11: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: This AC references 'the experiment results' and 'linked experiment work' but neither the experiments nor their outputs appear in the plan body — there is no Design summary section describing what experiments will be run or what their results are. The plan body mentions 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' but treats the experiments as already completed/exogenous. If the experiments are in-scope for this epic, there is no described work for them; if they are out-of-scope, the AC may not be satisfiable within the epic. Additionally, the AC list and Design summary both describe 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' consistently, and the six Success Criteria map cleanly to the twelve ACs with no obvious orphans. The only gap is the experiment work referenced but not described.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) add a section to the plan body describing the experiment work (what inputs, what metrics, what conclusion feeds the default), or (b) change the AC to 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth are documented with rationale; empirical calibration is a follow-on (F-experiments)' and note the experiments are deferred.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation' bullet and AC item 4: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record (extends the close-gate signing).'
findings.finding: The plan states the attestation mechanism '(extends the close-gate signing)', implying a signing infrastructure already exists in the close gate. Without codebase access it cannot be confirmed whether the close-gate signing is actually reusable as-is or will require significant re-engineering. If the close-gate signing is not easily extensible, implementing a parallel signing approach risks NIH duplication. This is flagged AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL because the plan explicitly intends to reuse the existing mechanism.
findings.suggested_edit: Reference the specific close-gate signing module/function name and describe the concrete extension point (e.g., 'we will call sign_attestation(ticket_id, commit_hash, coverage) already used in close-gate'). This removes ambiguity and confirms the plan is extending, not duplicating.

findings.location: AC item 11: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'; AC item 1: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open' (comprehensive list of tools and criteria)
findings.finding: Several ACs are difficult to verify within a single agent session: (1) 'Defaults grounded in experiment results' — the experiments are described as needed but not scoped, so verification depends on running them first; their runtime is unknown. (2) The combined scope of Layer-1 (8 distinct checks involving LSP, ast-grep, syft, registry calls, scc, lizard, DAG validity) plus Layer-2 (11+ advisory criteria plus triggered overlays, bounded broad pass) plus orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, config, sidecar event, and docs is extremely large. Whether one agent can complete all this in a single session is uncertain. The plan itself anticipates this risk ('Routing pivots on complexity + has_children') but does not decompose the epic into verifiable child tickets here — those are presumably the children referenced by the epic structure. If this ticket IS intended to be a parent/container epic, the 'completability' concern is lower; if it is meant to be executed directly, the scope is likely too large for a single session.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the plan whether this is a container epic (with children that do the actual work) or a directly executable ticket. If it is a container, add an AC like 'All described child tickets are filed, linked, and their union covers all ACs listed here.' If it is meant to be executed directly, decompose it.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria

Design summary — 'Trigger / enforcement' bullet: 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation'; AC item 5: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'
findings.finding: The plan names the `claim` command as the integration point but does not identify which file(s) in the rebar codebase implement `claim` or what the call signature looks like. The blast radius of modifying `claim` (e.g., does it touch a hot path, are there other callers or tests?) is unacknowledged. Similarly, 'reducer-ignored SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event' implies the event reducer needs modification (a skip/no-op branch), but the reducer file is not named.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the specific modules/files to be modified: the `claim` command handler, the event reducer (to add the skip for REVIEW_RESULT), and the attestation storage location. A one-line blast-radius acknowledgement ('only `claim` calls the gate; reducer change is additive') would satisfy the criterion.

findings.location: Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria sections collectively
Acceptance Criteria — no testing-plan AC present; Design summary — no testing section
findings.finding: The plan has no testing plan. There is no AC or design section describing how the gate itself will be tested — no mention of unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks, no integration tests for the claim→gate→attestation flow, no contract tests for the polyglot tool wrappers (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft), no failure-path coverage (what happens when a tool crashes or times out), and no RED-before-GREEN ordering. For a gate that will block agents from claiming tickets, the absence of a testing strategy is a significant gap: a false-positive bug in Layer-1 would silently block all work.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC (and a testing-plan section): 'Tests cover: (a) each Layer-1 check with a passing and a failing fixture; (b) fail-open behavior — tool crash/timeout → PASS, never FAIL — verified with mock tool stubs; (c) the claim fast-path with a valid, stale, and absent attestation; (d) the --force override records a justification event; (e) the REVIEW_RESULT event is emitted and the reducer ignores it. Tests must not require a running LSP server in CI (use stubs).'

findings.location: Full plan
Acceptance Criteria section, Success Criteria section, and Design summary — no testing-plan section exists anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan describes a sophisticated new subsystem (plan-review gate, Layer-1 deterministic checks, Layer-2 advisory LLM review, attestation signing, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events, claim integration, config surface) but contains zero description of how the implementation itself will be tested. There is no mention of: unit tests for individual criteria implementations, integration tests for the open→in_progress gate flow, contract/behavioral tests for the attestation signing and invalidation logic, tests for fail-open behavior (what happens when LSP/ast-grep/syft are unavailable), tests for the --force override path, tests for the REVIEW_RESULT event emission and reducer-skip behavior, RED-before-GREEN ordering for any of these, or boundary/failure-path cases (e.g., malformed ticket, tool crash, budget exhaustion leading to INDETERMINATE). The Acceptance Criteria list 12 items but none is a testing AC. The Follow-on list does not cover testing either. The phrase 'defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' implies experiments exist but does not constitute a test plan for the implementation.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Testing Plan' section (or a 13th AC) that covers at minimum: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 criterion (including fail-open / tool-unavailable path → PASS, not FAIL); (2) Unit/integration tests for Layer-2 orchestrator — mock LLM responses, verify per-criterion verdict parsing, AMBIGUOUS escalation path; (3) Integration test for the full open→in_progress gate: valid attestation passes, absent attestation blocks, material-edit-invalidated attestation blocks, --force override is recorded; (4) Attestation signing + invalidation: add a material edit, assert signature is invalidated; (5) REVIEW_RESULT event: assert reducer ignores the event type and the event payload contains required fingerprint fields; (6) Polyglot fail-open: simulate unsupported language / tool crash → assert criterion is skipped (PASS) not failed; (7) RED tests written before the corresponding implementation (RED-before-GREEN).

findings.location: Design summary ('Execution' bullet), Acceptance Criteria ('Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing… per-plan budget cap… INDETERMINATE outcome'), and Success Criteria ('defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results')
findings.finding: The plan's stated end-state is well-defined across the six Success Criteria and twelve Acceptance Criteria, and the described work (Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim integration, config, sidecar event, docs) maps onto those ACs in a generally coherent way. However, one gap exists that cannot be resolved without codebase access: the AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on results from an external linked experiment. If those experiments have not yet been run or their outputs are not yet available, the agent cannot reach a 'grounded defaults' end-state within this epic — producing either ungrounded defaults (which violates the AC) or an incomplete epic. The plan does not clarify whether the experiments are a prerequisite, a parallel work stream, or already complete. Additionally, the termination condition for the out-of-band review loop is described in the design summary ('client revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature') but is not captured as an explicit AC, leaving the loop-exit condition slightly underspecified for the implementing agent.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify the status of the linked experiment work: (a) if experiments are a prerequisite, add an explicit dependency AC or note that defaults will be placeholder values pending experiment results; (b) if already complete, reference their outputs directly in the AC. Optionally strengthen the loop-exit AC to explicitly state: 'The claim gate exits the review loop when a valid non-stale signed attestation is present; a material edit re-enters the loop; --force bypasses with a recorded justification.'

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' bullet; AC bullet 1 ('solidlsp (already in repo)', 'ast-grep', 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback', 'scc + lizard')
findings.finding: The plan asserts several tools are already present or available without verification: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — only confirmable against the live codebase; (2) 'ast-grep' — assumed installable/available in the execution environment; (3) 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback' — registry API availability and credentials are assumed; (4) 'rebar.llm extension points' ('reusing rebar.llm extension points') — assumes the catalog/contracts/prompt/runner interfaces already exist and are compatible. Additionally, the plan references 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' as the plan-of-record and claims defaults will be 'grounded in the experiment results' — both are external dependencies the executor must locate and trust. None of these are wrong, but each is an assumption an executor needs to verify before relying on it.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified pre-conditions' section listing: which tools are confirmed in the repo vs. must be added; which rebar.llm interfaces already exist; where the session log and experiment results are stored. This surfaces the assumptions explicitly so the executor can validate them at start.

findings.location: Context section; Design summary first paragraph
AC bullet 11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results') vs. Follow-on F1/F2/F3/F4; Success Criteria SC4 vs. AC bullet 4 + AC bullet 6
findings.finding: Two potential orphan/gap issues: (1) AC bullet 11 requires defaults to be 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — but the plan does not describe what those experiments are, where they live, or who runs them. If the experiments are not already complete, this AC has no corresponding described work and would be unreachable. (2) The plan's Design summary prominently describes the 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event' and 'Observability/FP substrate' sub-system; this maps to SC5 and AC bullet 8, so coverage exists, but there is no AC bullet explicitly covering the 'reducer skips the type' property — it appears only parenthetically in AC bullet 8. Minor gap but worth making explicit. Terminology is mostly consistent; 'INDETERMINATE' is used in both the design and ACs coherently. No outright duplicate criteria found.
findings.suggested_edit: For AC bullet 11: either confirm the linked experiments are pre-existing artifacts the executor can read, or add a sub-task that runs them as part of this epic. For the reducer-skipping property: add it explicitly to AC bullet 8 rather than burying it in parentheses.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'
findings.finding: The plan calls for a 'criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator' reusing existing rebar.llm extension points. This is sound if those extension points genuinely exist and are already used by the completion-verifier (no new abstraction layer). However, if the orchestrator/registry pattern is being introduced here for the first time, it would be a new abstraction layer used at a single call-site in this epic — a potential Rule-of-Three concern. This cannot be confirmed without the live codebase. Additionally, the 'per-criterion config knob' (advisory | blocking-with-override) combined with per-plan budget cap, chunking config, depth-routing config, and escalation config adds a significant configuration surface; the plan should verify none of these overlap with existing rebar configuration primitives.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm (in pre-conditions or design note) that the catalog/contracts/prompt/runner interfaces already exist in rebar.llm and are already used by at least one other gate. If they don't exist yet, evaluate whether a lighter integration suffices for v1.

findings.location: AC bullet 11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'); AC bullet 6 ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)')
findings.finding: Two measurability concerns: (1) AC bullet 11 defers to 'experiment results' from a linked work item. If those experiments have not yet been run and recorded, the AC cannot be verified within the session executing this ticket — the agent would need to wait for external results. This makes the criterion potentially out-of-session. (2) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' is mentioned as input to escalation decisions, but no definition or measurement protocol for 'centrality' is given. Without an operationalised definition (e.g., 'number of direct callers of changed symbols via LSP/ast-grep'), an agent cannot verify this criterion. Additionally, 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' in SC3 is not directly measurable within the session (it implies observing downstream agent behaviour).
findings.suggested_edit: For AC bullet 11: either scope it to 'defaults are documented and justified by reference to the linked experiment log' (verifiable by inspection) or move it to a follow-on. For 'centrality': add a 1-sentence operational definition (e.g., 'number of direct importers of affected symbols'). For SC3: reword to 'findings are grounded, specific, severity-ranked, and checklist-form per the coaching spec' — verifiable by inspection.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria

Design summary — 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. `claim` is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation'; AC bullet 5 ('`claim` integration'); AC bullet 4 ('Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record (extends the close-gate signing)')
findings.finding: The plan references the existing `claim` command and the existing close-gate signing mechanism ('extends the close-gate signing') as touch-points but does not name specific files, modules, or symbols to be modified. The blast radius of modifying `claim` (and whatever event-reducer handles STATUS transitions) is non-trivial — callers of `claim`, tests of `claim`, the reducer for the open→in_progress transition, and any CI integrations that read ticket state could all be affected. The plan acknowledges the close-gate analogue but does not enumerate which close-gate modules will be reused or extended. Without codebase access this cannot be confirmed as wrong, but the absence of named files/modules is a gap for the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Files/modules to touch' list naming at minimum: the `claim` command module, the close-gate signing module being extended, the event reducer that will skip `REVIEW_RESULT`, and the config surface file. Reference these by their actual repo paths if known.

findings.location: Success Criteria (6 items); Acceptance Criteria (12 items); Design summary 'Attestation' + 'Trigger/enforcement' bullets
Acceptance Criteria — all 12 bullets; Success Criteria — all 6 bullets
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at all. There is no AC, no Success Criterion, and no Design summary mention of: (a) how Layer-1 deterministic checks will be tested (e.g., unit tests with synthetic ticket fixtures for each check type); (b) how Layer-2 advisory criteria will be tested (e.g., golden-set plan fixtures with expected per-criterion verdicts); (c) failure-path coverage for the fail-open behaviour (a tool crash/timeout must yield PASS, not FAIL — this is a critical invariant with no stated test); (d) integration test for the `claim` gate (does absence of attestation actually block?); (e) the signing/invalidation logic (material-edit should invalidate; non-material edit should not); (f) RED-before-GREEN ordering or TDD mention. This is a significant omission for a feature of this complexity.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC bullet (or a dedicated 'Testing' section) covering at minimum: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check with pass/fail/skip fixtures; (2) at least one golden-set integration test for the full review pipeline; (3) explicit test of fail-open: tool crash → criterion PASS; (4) test of `claim` blocking on missing/stale attestation; (5) test of material-edit invalidation. Note that structural grep / command-output assertions are acceptable cross-language test patterns here.

findings.location: Design summary 'Trigger/enforcement' bullet vs. AC bullet 6; AC bullet 11 vs. Follow-on work
findings.finding: Two potential internal tensions: (1) The Design summary says 'the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' and 'a REVIEW PROCESS, not a dialog — the client revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature.' AC bullet 6 says defaults are 'grounded in experiment results (see linked experiment work).' If those experiments are also out-of-band and not yet complete, the executor cannot satisfy AC bullet 11 within this epic — potentially creating a circular dependency where the epic's defaults can't be finalized until experiments from another work item complete. (2) The Follow-on list F11 is 'rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers', but SC6 says 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal' — implying the CI-check pattern is in-scope (also backed by AC bullet 12 'Docs: … the CI-check pattern'). There is a mild tension between the docs-only scoping of CI in this epic vs. the helpers being deferred to F11; this is not a contradiction but should be made explicit.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the plan whether (a) the experiment results pre-exist or must be produced as part of this epic; and (b) whether 'CI-check pattern' in Docs means documentation only (no tooling), with actual helpers deferred to F11.

findings.location: Plan description, NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT section; SC2 ('all review-enforcing CI wrappers fail-closed … in ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml'); SC3 ('retains llm-review'); SC4 ('commit whose diff is entirely within the event-sourced ticket store is exempt'); SC5 ('removed from the review-gate allowlist')
findings.finding:
The plan makes several structural claims about the existing codebase that can only be confirmed against the live repo, not the plan text alone:

1. **Three named CI workflow files exist** (`review-coverage-invariant.yml`, `dangling-references.yml`, `ruleset-invariants.yml`) and each currently contains (or will be given) fail-closed precondition/token guards. The plan treats this as a given in SC2 without noting it as something to verify/create.
2. **`llm-review` is already a required check** on the live `main` ruleset (SC3 says 'retains llm-review'). If it has already been removed or renamed, the swap in Story 7 silently omits a protection.
3. **An 'event-sourced ticket store' path boundary exists** and the coverage logic can detect 'diff entirely within' it (SC4). The plan neither defines the path glob nor confirms any existing allowlist mechanism handles it.
4. **A `review-gate` check name is not yet required** — the plan assumes this name is free and not already claimed by another workflow that could satisfy or interfere with the new gate.
5. **`required-checks.txt` is the authoritative local mirror** of the live ruleset (SC3). Its current state and sync mechanism are assumed to exist without citation.

An executor relying on any of these without first verifying them in the live repo risks building against a false baseline (e.g., wiring fail-closed logic into a workflow file that does not yet exist, or omitting a check that was already dropped from the ruleset).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Story 0 (or a pre-flight checklist at the top of the epic) that explicitly enumerates and verifies: (a) the three CI workflow files exist and their current token-guard logic; (b) the current live required-checks on the main ruleset (admin PAT read); (c) the exact path glob for the ticket store and its current allowlist entry; (d) confirm `review-gate` is not an existing check name. Record outcomes in a short verification artifact before Stories 1-4 begin.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA (SC1-SC6); DELIVERY PRINCIPLES ('Warn-first … validated on live PR2 traffic BEFORE any enforce-flip'); experiment references E2/E5/E6'/E8/E11/E13/E14/E15/V0.3a-post-fix/V0.11/R8
SEQUENCING section vs. SC2/SC3 success criteria
findings.finding: The sequencing states 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel after start; story 5 depends on 1+2+4; story 6 depends on 5; story 7 (enforce-flip) depends on 1-6 ALL verified; story 8 (UX) is independent.' However, SC3 requires the enforce-flip (story 7) to swap 'merge-pipeline-checks -> review-gate' in BOTH required-checks.txt AND the live ruleset, and SC2 requires all three carriers (review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml) to be fail-closed BEFORE the enforce-flip. Story 3 (which presumably covers ruleset-invariants.yml) is listed as parallelizable with stories 1, 2, and 4 — but without individual story definitions in the plan text, it cannot be confirmed that story 3 is a prerequisite of story 7. If story 3 is NOT included in the 'stories 1-6 ALL verified' gate for story 7, the enforce-flip could proceed without the R8 fail-closed guard in place, violating SC2/SC3. The individual story definitions are referenced but not reproduced here, making this impossible to fully verify from the plan alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly enumerate, inline in the SEQUENCING section, that story 7's dependency on 'stories 1-6 ALL verified' specifically requires SC2 pass (all three carriers fail-closed) and SC3 pass (live ruleset parity guard active). Add a checklist item under story 7's prerequisites: 'R8 fail-closed guard verified on ruleset-invariants.yml (SC2), live ruleset reflects the swap (SC3).'

findings.location: Multiple sections — story definitions, 'warn-first' principle, course-correction triggers, and enforce-flip runbook
findings.finding:
The plan references individual stories (1–8) and their contents extensively (e.g., 'each story names a COURSE-CORRECTION TRIGGER', 'the enforce-flip story carries the runbook', story-level 'dependency links', 'E-series/V-series experiments') but does NOT include the actual story definitions, experiment definitions, or runbook text anywhere in the reproduced plan. An executing agent would be forced to stop and locate docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md and docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md before being able to act on any story. Concretely:
1. No story bodies are present — the agent cannot determine what code/config changes each story requires.
2. COURSE-CORRECTION TRIGGERs are promised per story but are absent — the agent cannot know when to stop and re-plan.
3. The enforce-flip runbook ('land the swap with no other PR2 in flight — R8 RED is repo-wide during the live<->file skew window') is mentioned but not included; the agent cannot execute the flip safely.
4. Experiment references (E2, E5, E6', E8, E11, E13, E14, E15, V0.3a-post-fix, V0.11, V0.12, R8) are cited as verification steps in every SC but are not defined inline — the agent cannot verify the SCs without them.
5. The 'allowlist' mentioned in SC1 and SC5 is not defined (which paths/patterns are allowlisted vs. reviewable?).
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline the full story definitions, experiment specs, course-correction triggers, and the enforce-flip runbook into this ticket, or (b) add a mandatory pre-execution step: 'Executor must confirm access to docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md and docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md and extract the story bodies, experiment definitions, and runbook before beginning any story.' At minimum, define the allowlist contents (SC1/SC5) and the enforce-flip runbook steps inline, since those are the highest-risk, hardest-to-reverse elements.

findings.location: Plan body — references to 'review-coverage-invariant.yml', 'dangling-references.yml', 'ruleset-invariants.yml', 'required-checks.txt', 'docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md', 'docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md'
findings.finding: The plan names several specific files and CI workflow YAMLs (review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml, required-checks.txt, option-a-pivot-plan.md, ADR-0020) as the principal edit targets, but it does not enumerate the full set of callers or consumers that would need to change alongside them. Specifically: (1) Any workflow or job that currently references the old 'merge-pipeline-checks' required check by name (which is being swapped to 'review-gate') would need to be updated — these callers are not listed. (2) The 'allowlist' for the review gate is mentioned (SC1, SC5) but neither its current location/filename nor which story touches it is called out. (3) 'Coverage ledger' is referenced (SC4) without naming the file/store that backs it. (4) ADR-0020 is listed as a source-of-truth document and as something to be 'updated with execution results' (SC6), but no story explicitly owns that update. These gaps are not definitively wrong — the live codebase may already contain all these files exactly as named — but they cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone, so the blast radius cannot be fully assessed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / symbols touched' table to the plan (or to each story) that lists: (a) every workflow YAML being created or modified, (b) the allowlist file path, (c) the coverage-ledger file/store, (d) any composite-action or reusable-workflow files, and (e) any documentation/config files beyond ADR-0020. For the merge-pipeline-checks → review-gate rename, explicitly list all callers (workflow 'needs:' references, branch-protection API calls, etc.) that must be updated atomically with the rename.

findings.location: Title, NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT, SUCCESS CRITERIA, DELIVERY PRINCIPLES, SEQUENCING sections

Design summary ('Two layers' + 'Verdict model') vs. AC ('Layer-1 deterministic criteria…readiness shape, file/symbol/import resolution, package existence, oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff')
findings.finding: The design summary says Layer-1 'BLOCKS' on sound, unambiguous checks, and the success criteria confirm Layer-1 'blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks'. However, several items listed under Layer-1 in the AC — 'AC/DD quality' and 'destructive sniff' — involve judgment rather than deterministic evaluation, creating a tension with the claim that Layer-1 is 'deterministic'. It is plausible that these are defined as simple heuristics (e.g., regex sniff), but the plan does not say so explicitly. This may be a genuine contradiction or just an under-specified boundary. Cannot resolve without knowing how 'AC/DD quality' and 'destructive sniff' are evaluated deterministically.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence in the Layer-1 description clarifying what 'deterministic' means for each listed check — e.g., 'AC/DD quality = presence of a standardized ## Acceptance Criteria heading (structural, not judgment); destructive sniff = regex/AST pattern match for DROP TABLE / rm -rf / etc.'

findings.location: Design summary ('Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers)'); AC ('Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points')
findings.finding: Three unverified premises an executor would need to check before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — executor will assume LSP tooling exists and is wired up; if absent it changes scope significantly. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' — assumes those extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) already exist and are flexible enough to host criteria as declarative descriptors without modification. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes close-gate signing code is factored to be reusable/extendable rather than inline. All three are correctness-relevant for scoping and could require rework if wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Verified prerequisites' subsection listing: (a) solidlsp is present and functional in the repo, (b) rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner as stable extension points, (c) close-gate signing is factored into a reusable component. Mark each as confirmed or as a spike task.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: This criterion defers measurability to an external artifact: 'see linked experiment work'. An executing agent cannot verify within the session that the defaults are 'grounded in experiment results' unless the experiments are defined, runnable, and their outputs are available. If the experiment work is a separate ticket/epic (as implied), this criterion is not completable within the scope of this ticket — it depends on a prior deliverable. The criterion as written is not independently verifiable.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) link explicitly to the completed experiment ticket and state what specific numeric results the defaults must match (e.g., 'chunk size = N criteria per call as established in experiment ticket #XYZ'), or (b) move this criterion to the experiment ticket and replace it here with 'Defaults are configurable and documented; initial values are placeholders pending experiment results (tracked in #XYZ)'.

findings.location: Plan title ('Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)') and AC list (12 distinct ACs spanning infra, layer-1, layer-2, orchestration, attestation, claim integration, config, observability, docs)
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic, but it is presented as a single executable unit. The AC list spans at least 7–8 independently shippable subsystems: (1) Layer-1 deterministic checks + polyglot tooling, (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria + orchestrator, (3) criteria registry + generic runner, (4) attestation/signing subsystem, (5) claim integration, (6) config surface, (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer exemption, (8) docs. No child ticket decomposition is included in the plan itself; the follow-on section lists F1–F12 but those are post-epic work. An agent assigned this ticket would have no decomposition to execute against — the plan's own language ('This epic builds...') confirms it is not a leaf. The absence of a child breakdown is a real gap for executability.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Child tickets' section enumerating at least the 7-8 subsystems above as separate leaf tickets (each with their own ACs), and confirm that *this* ticket's ACs are satisfied when all children are done. Alternatively, if this is intentionally a design/planning epic (output = a decomposition), reframe the ACs accordingly.

findings.location: Design summary ('Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type') vs. AC ('Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)') vs. AC ('Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children)')
findings.finding: Two small terminology/coverage gaps: (1) The design summary says routing pivots on 'has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type', but the AC explicitly exempts bugs by ticket type. These are not strictly contradictory (bugs could be exempted before routing), but the relationship is not stated. (2) 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' appears in the design summary and AC ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality') but there is no corresponding AC item defining what 'centrality' means, how it is computed, or what its output looks like — it is described but not acceptance-tested. Overall coherence is good; these are gaps rather than contradictions.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC sub-bullet clarifying: 'Bugs are exempted upstream of the routing step (pre-filter), consistent with routing-by-complexity not ticket-type.' Add a measurable definition of 'centrality' (e.g., 'number of reverse dependencies as computed by ast-grep callers') to the escalation AC.

findings.location: Multiple locations: Design summary ('Execution: chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB… default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'); AC ('Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality'); AC ('Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec')
findings.finding: Several design decisions are explicitly deferred to the executor: (1) Chunking defaults are 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' with no current values given — executor must run experiments to determine them. (2) 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' does not specify the algorithm or data source. (3) 'Escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' does not define the threshold function. (4) 'Coaching spec' is referenced but not included — the spec that governs feedback authoring is not reproduced or linked in a recoverable way (the session log reference '63cc-ec94-4792-464b' may not be accessible to an executing agent). These are real design decisions pushed onto the executor, not implementable without further clarification.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred decision, either inline the decision or link to a co-located spec document: (a) provide placeholder chunking defaults with a note they are provisional, (b) define centrality metric concretely, (c) define escalation thresholds (even if provisional), (d) reproduce or attach the coaching spec inline or in a linked appendix reachable without the session log.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound before the agent executes it… ~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'

Design summary ('Polyglot tooling: solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep'); AC ('claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'); AC ('REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event… reducer skips the type')
findings.finding: The plan names several integration points — the `claim` command, the event reducer, the close-gate signing module, rebar.llm extension points — but does not enumerate which files/modules these live in. An executor would need to discover them. For a plan of this scale, the absence of even rough file targets (e.g., 'rebar/claim.py', 'rebar/events/reducer.py') means the blast radius is unacknowledged. It is plausible that rebar has a well-known structure where these are obvious, but that cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief 'Affected modules' table listing the known integration touch-points (claim command, event reducer, close-gate signing, rebar.llm orchestrator) with their approximate file paths, and note that a full blast-radius scan should be done at execution start.

findings.location: Title vs. Design summary vs. AC
findings.finding: The plan title ('Plan-review verification gate open→in_progress'), the context, the design summary, and the ACs are all consistently scoped to a plan-review gate at claim time. No scope drift or contradiction between headline and detail detected. The follow-on section cleanly separates out-of-scope items.

findings.location: Design summary ('Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'); AC ('Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator')
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'criteria registry' as a new declarative descriptor system and a 'generic orchestrator', and explicitly states it reuses rebar.llm extension points. Without knowing the current shape of rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, it is unclear whether the registry is a genuine reuse of existing machinery or a parallel structure that duplicates it. If rebar.llm already has a fully functional criteria/prompt orchestration loop, adding a separate 'criteria registry' could be NIH. Cannot confirm from plan text alone — needs codebase inspection.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence clarifying the relationship between the new criteria registry and rebar.llm's existing catalog: 'The criteria registry IS rebar.llm's catalog extended with plan-review descriptor fields' (if reuse) vs. 'The criteria registry is a new component that calls rebar.llm's runner as a backend' (if new layer). This prevents the executor from building a duplicate.

findings.location: Multiple sections: SC1 ('per-SHA coverage'), SC2 ('ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml'), SC3 ('live main ruleset requires review-gate and retains llm-review'), SC4 ('coverage ledger warm-hits across re-runs'), SC5 ('removed from the review-gate allowlist')
findings.finding:
The plan makes several structural claims about the codebase that an executor would need to verify before relying on them, and these cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone:

1. **Existing CI file names and their current behaviour**: SC2 asserts that three specific YAML files (`review-coverage-invariant.yml`, `dangling-references.yml`, `ruleset-invariants.yml`) each have a wrapper that must be made fail-closed. The plan does not confirm these files exist, what their current exit-code behaviour is, or whether they already have a wrapper stub waiting for modification.

2. **'Coverage ledger warm-hits across re-runs' (SC4)**: The plan treats a warm-hit caching layer for the SHA-coverage ledger as already present ('no flaky-check team block'), but does not say whether this cache/ledger exists today, where it lives, or what its invalidation contract is.

3. **'review-gate allowlist' (SC5)**: SC5 says behavior-bearing surfaces are 'removed from the review-gate allowlist', implying an allowlist already exists in the codebase. Its current contents, format, and location are unspecified.

4. **'retains llm-review' (SC3)**: SC3 asserts the live ruleset currently contains a `llm-review` required check that must be retained. Whether this check is present in the live ruleset today is an environmental fact that cannot be confirmed from the plan.

5. **'merge-pipeline-checks → review-gate swap' (SC3)**: The plan implies a check named `merge-pipeline-checks` is currently in the ruleset and that swapping it for `review-gate` is the intended change. This assumption about the current ruleset state is unverifiable without live access.

None of these is necessarily wrong, but each is an untested premise. If any of the files are absent, renamed, or have different semantics, the executor will discover it mid-story and stall.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Prerequisites / verified-state' section (or a checklist at the top of each story) that requires the executor to confirm: (a) the three CI YAML files exist and their current exit-code behaviour; (b) the coverage ledger mechanism (type, location, current cache key); (c) the current allowlist file path and representative entries; (d) the live ruleset's current required-check names (screenshot or `gh api` output). This makes each assumption explicit and falsifiable before story work begins.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA (SC1–SC6), DELIVERY PRINCIPLES ('Live-verify each story on real GitHub … AND by the named E-series/V-series experiments'), individual experiment references [E2, E5, E6', E8, E11, E13, E14, E15, V0.3a-post-fix, V0.11, R8]
findings.finding:
The plan's testing strategy is defined almost entirely by reference to experiment/verification tags (E2, E8, E11, E13–E15, V0.3a-post-fix, V0.11, R8) whose detailed protocols live in an external document (`docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md`) that is not reproduced here. The plan text alone therefore has the following gaps:

1. **RED-before-GREEN ordering**: SC1 and SC2 describe desired end-states (gate must block, token failure must be RED) but neither section nor any named story explicitly states that a failing-state test must be run *before* the fix is merged to confirm the check was genuinely red. This is implied by 'live-verify on real PR2 traffic' but is not made explicit.

2. **Boundary / edge-case coverage for SC4**: SC4 names three positive pass-cases (clean PR, ticket-store-only commit, warm re-run) but does not specify how a *partial* coverage scenario is tested — e.g. a PR whose HEAD contains one reviewed SHA and one un-reviewed SHA. This is a boundary case directly relevant to the 'per-SHA coverage' contract in SC1.

3. **Failure-path coverage for the warn→enforce transition**: The plan states that each story ships in warn mode first, but the criteria for what constitutes a passing warn-mode verification (vs. a false-quiet advisory) are not spelled out. An executor cannot tell when warn-mode is 'good enough' to flip.

4. **Experiment content is opaque**: Because the test protocols are in a separate doc, a reviewer or executor reading only this plan cannot assess whether the named experiments cover the failure paths above. The plan is therefore AMBIGUOUS with respect to completeness — it may be fully adequate once cross-referenced, or it may be thin.

The structural grep / command-output assertions pattern is explicitly accepted per the criterion note, and the plan does use live-GitHub verification as a legitimate test mechanism, which is a strength.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline a minimal summary of each named experiment's pass/fail observable directly in the story's AC (e.g. 'E13 PASS = a PR2 carrying SHA X with no review entry is blocked at the gate with exit code 1, not 0'), or (b) add an explicit RED-before-GREEN step to SC1/SC2 ('before the fix lands, confirm the gate exits non-zero on the un-fixed branch'), and (c) add a boundary-case test for partial per-SHA coverage to SC4.

findings.location: SEQUENCING section: 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel after start; story 5 depends on 1+2+4; story 6 depends on 5; story 7 (enforce-flip) depends on 1-6 ALL verified; story 8 (UX) is independent' vs. SC3 and the NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT
findings.finding:
Three potential internal tensions are present, none critical but collectively worth flagging:

1. SEQUENCING says stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel, but SC3 requires both 'required-checks.txt AND the live ruleset' to be updated atomically (the 'live<->file skew window' concern mentioned under Reversibility). If story 3 (or whichever story manages required-checks.txt) and the story managing the live ruleset are allowed to land independently in parallel, there is a sequencing window where the file and the live ruleset are temporarily out of sync — which the plan elsewhere treats as a dangerous state ('R8 RED is repo-wide during the live<->file skew window'). The plan does not name which story(ies) own each half of SC3, so it cannot be verified that the parallel-run permission is safe.

2. The Warn-first principle says 'every story ships its enforcing logic in warn/advisory mode… BEFORE any enforce-flip' and 'the enforce-flip story carries the runbook'. SC6 says 'the two-tier flow is retained and its UX correctness fixes land'. Story 8 (UX) is declared independent and may land 'any time', including after the enforce-flip (story 7). If UX story 8 modifies PR titles or the sprint draft-umbrella lifecycle after enforce is live, a partially-broken UX state could exist under a hard gate — contradicting the spirit of warn-first.

3. SC2 says fail-closed behavior must be verified 'in ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml', but the NON-NEGOTIABLE says the enforcing checks must be fail-closed. The plan never explicitly says which story is responsible for each carrier file, making it ambiguous whether all three are covered before story 7's dependency check ('1-6 ALL verified').
findings.suggested_edit:
1. In SEQUENCING, explicitly note that the two half-stories (or story sub-tasks) that together form SC3's atomic swap of required-checks.txt + live ruleset must land in a single coordinated step (not as independent parallel work-items), or add an explicit ordering constraint between them.
2. Constrain story 8 to 'must land before story 7 (enforce-flip)' or explicitly acknowledge the risk of a post-enforce UX gap.
3. Add a cross-reference table mapping each of the three carrier files (review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml) to the story responsible for its fail-closed behavior, so the dependency check for story 7 is unambiguous.

findings.location: Multiple sections: NON-NEGOTIABLE, SC1, SC2, SC3, SC4, SC5, SEQUENCING
findings.finding:
The plan depends heavily on external referenced documents ('docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md', 'docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md') for the actual design detail — Phase 2 (§5), review-#4/#6 prerequisites, experiments V0.1-V0.12, E-series/V-series experiment definitions. The plan body itself does not reproduce the content of those sources. An executing agent that cannot access those files would be blocked at virtually every story, because:

- SC1 references 'per-SHA coverage' logic and experiments E2/E13 without defining what those experiments require the implementation to do.
- SC2 references 'V0.3a-post-fix' and 'R8 fail-closed' without stating what V0.3a-post-fix established as the accepted behavior.
- SC3 references 'E6\', V0.11' and 'required-checks.txt' without stating where that file lives or what its schema is.
- SC4 references 'E5/E8/E14/E15' and 'event-sourced ticket store' exemption without defining the ticket store path or exemption criteria.
- SC5 references 'E11/E-compute-hash' and 'behavior-bearing surfaces' vs 'genuinely-inert artifacts' without giving the definitive allowlist contents or the criterion for membership.
- SEQUENCING references 'story 1', 'story 2', etc. but no story definitions appear in the plan — the stories are not described anywhere in the plan text provided.

Additionally, several terms are used but not defined inline:
- 'two-tier flow' — the structure is referenced but never described.
- 'PR2' — appears repeatedly (e.g. 'live PR2 traffic', 'no other PR2 in flight') but is never defined.
- 'R8' — used as shorthand (e.g. 'R8 RED', 'R8 fail-closed', 'R8 live-parity guard') without definition.
- 'merge-pipeline-checks->review-gate swap' — the before/after state is not described inline.
- 'completion-verifier PASS' — referenced in the epic-close condition but not described.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline the minimum necessary design decisions from option-a-pivot-plan.md and ADR-0020 so an agent can act without fetching external docs (define PR1/PR2, R8, the stories, the allowlist, the exemption criteria, the experiment pass/fail conditions), or (b) add a 'Glossary / Inline Definitions' section that resolves every shorthand term and experiment reference used in the success criteria, and explicitly state that the referenced external documents are a prerequisite read before any story begins (making that a Story 0 / pre-flight step). At minimum, the individual story definitions must be present or linked with enough fidelity that the executor knows what to build in each story.

findings.location: Design summary ('Two layers' + 'Verdict model') vs. AC ('Layer-1 deterministic criteria ... fail-open') vs. Success Criteria SC-2
findings.finding: The plan states Layer-1 'BLOCKS' and Layer-2 is 'ADVISORY by default', yet the verdict model also introduces INDETERMINATE as 'non-blocking by default, project-configurable'.
